title: Introduce URI-versioned v2 orders API on Express without forking the v1 handler
business_context: A logistics SaaS serves 1,400 B2B integrators against /api/orders. Product wants to change the order line item shape (split `price` into `unit_price` + `currency`) but 600 integrators are still hard-coded to the flat shape and cannot be migrated in under 6 months. Breaking v1 would page on-call within minutes.
constraints: v1 response shape must stay byte-identical for existing clients
single codebase, no second deploy/service
peak 3,200 req/s across both versions
auth via existing JWT middleware unchanged
version negotiation must be observable per-client for deprecation tracking

before_state.description: One handler hard-codes the flat price shape; any change ripples to all clients.

issues: Single shape for all clients; cannot evolve `price` without breaking everyone
No version dimension in metrics, so deprecation impact is invisible
`price_cents/100` loses currency, blocking multi-currency expansion

reasoning: Naive approach: add `?v=2` query param and branch inside the handler with if/else on the price shape — rejected because the handler grows unbounded and v1/v2 logic interleave, making it impossible to freeze v1. Rejected alternative: deploy a second service for v2 — rejected because it duplicates auth, db pooling, and doubles infra for a shape change. Chosen: a serializer layer keyed by version. UI/client picks /api/v1 or /api/v2 prefix -> a version-resolver middleware tags req.apiVersion and increments a per-version counter -> a single controller fetches the order once from data -> a presenter map (v1Presenter, v2Presenter) shapes the response -> config holds the deprecation sunset date for v1 emitted as a header -> tests snapshot both presenters against the same fixture -> rollout flips clients by updating their base URL, tracked via the per-version metric.

after_state.description: Versioned routers share one controller; presenters own the shape; v1 is frozen and instrumented for deprecation.

files.purpose: Tags each request with its version, emits Deprecation/Sunset headers, and makes version usage observable for deprecation tracking.
Owns the per-version response shape so v1 stays frozen byte-for-byte while v2 carries currency.
Single controller fetches once; routers differ only by version middleware and mount prefix.
Mounts both versions behind shared auth so no auth logic is duplicated.

migration_strategy: Deploy v2 routes behind /api/v2 while all clients still call /api/v1; v2 traffic starts at zero
Publish OpenAPI for v2 and notify the 800 migratable integrators with the Sunset date
Migrate internal first-party callers to v2 and watch api_version_requests_total{version=v2} climb
Canary 5% of opt-in integrators; if v2 error rate exceeds v1 baseline, they revert by changing base URL back (no deploy)
Ramp remaining clients; alert when any client still hits v1 within 30 days of sunset
After sunset with v1 traffic at zero for 14 days, delete v1 router and v1Presenter
testing_strategy: Fixture: a real multi-currency order with price_cents and currency='EUR'. Assert v1Presenter output equals the frozen golden JSON (price as float, no currency) AND v2Presenter emits unit_price in cents + currency. Assert the metric counter increments with the correct {version} label (side-effect) by spying on counter.inc. Negative/chaos test: request /api/v1 and assert Deprecation:true + Sunset header present; request /api/v3 (unmounted) and assert 404 with no version counter increment.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The query-param if/else handler forces v1 and v2 serialization into one function; the first time someone edits the shared `items.map` for v2 they shift a field v1 clients parse positionally, and because there is no per-version metric the breakage is invisible until integrators open tickets.

title: Add cursor-based pagination to a FastAPI feed replacing fragile offset paging
business_context: A social app's /feed endpoint uses LIMIT/OFFSET. With 12M posts and active users scrolling, deep offsets (OFFSET 50000) take 4s and duplicate/skip posts when new posts arrive mid-scroll. The mobile team reports 9% of infinite-scroll sessions show repeated cards.
constraints: must keep offset paging working for the current app version during rollout
stable ordering under concurrent inserts
p95 page fetch under 150ms at any depth
cursor must be opaque and tamper-evident (clients must not forge it)
Postgres single primary, read replicas allowed

before_state.description: Offset paging re-scans rows and drifts when new posts are inserted between requests.

files.content:
from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends
from .db import database

router = APIRouter()

@router.get('/feed')
async def feed(offset: int = 0, limit: int = 20):
    rows = await database.fetch_all(
        'SELECT id, author, body, created_at FROM posts '
        'ORDER BY created_at DESC OFFSET :o LIMIT :l',
        {'o': offset, 'l': limit})
    return {'items': [dict(r) for r in rows], 'next_offset': offset + limit}
issues: OFFSET 50000 scans and discards 50k rows -> 4s latency
New inserts shift the window, causing duplicate and skipped posts
created_at is not unique, so rows at the same timestamp reorder between pages

reasoning: Naive approach: cap offset and tell clients to stop scrolling — rejected because it just hides the bug and breaks the product's infinite feed. Rejected alternative: keyset on created_at alone — rejected because created_at collisions reorder ties across pages, still duplicating posts. Chosen: keyset pagination on the composite (created_at, id) with an opaque HMAC-signed cursor. UI sends `cursor` -> API decodes+verifies HMAC -> data query uses WHERE (created_at,id) < (:ts,:id) on a composite index -> response returns next cursor encoding the last row's (ts,id) -> config holds the cursor signing secret -> tests assert no dupes across a concurrent insert -> rollout keeps offset param alive until old app versions age out.

after_state.description: Keyset paging on a composite index with signed cursors; stable under concurrent inserts.

files.content:
import base64, hmac, hashlib, json, os

_SECRET = os.environ['FEED_CURSOR_SECRET'].encode()

def encode(created_at: str, post_id: int) -> str:
    payload = json.dumps({'t': created_at, 'i': post_id}).encode()
    sig = hmac.new(_SECRET, payload, hashlib.sha256).digest()[:16]
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(sig + payload).decode()

def decode(cursor: str):
    raw = base64.urlsafe_b64decode(cursor)
    sig, payload = raw[:16], raw[16:]
    expected = hmac.new(_SECRET, payload, hashlib.sha256).digest()[:16]
    if not hmac.compare_digest(sig, expected):
        raise ValueError('bad_cursor')
    d = json.loads(payload)
    return d['t'], d['i']
files.purpose: Opaque, tamper-evident cursor: HMAC stops clients forging positions while keeping the value opaque.

files.content:
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_posts_feed
  ON posts (created_at DESC, id DESC);
files.purpose: Composite index that makes keyset comparison an index range scan at any depth.

files.content:
from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
from .db import database
from . import cursor as cur

router = APIRouter()

@router.get('/feed')
async def feed(cursor: str | None = None, offset: int | None = None, limit: int = 20):
    limit = min(limit, 100)
    if cursor:
        ts, pid = cur.decode(cursor)
        rows = await database.fetch_all(
            'SELECT id, author, body, created_at FROM posts '
            'WHERE (created_at, id) < (:t, :i) '
            'ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC LIMIT :l',
            {'t': ts, 'i': pid, 'l': limit})
    elif offset is not None:
        rows = await database.fetch_all(
            'SELECT id, author, body, created_at FROM posts '
            'ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC OFFSET :o LIMIT :l',
            {'o': offset, 'l': limit})
    else:
        rows = await database.fetch_all(
            'SELECT id, author, body, created_at FROM posts '
            'ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC LIMIT :l', {'l': limit})
    items = [dict(r) for r in rows]
    next_cursor = cur.encode(str(items[-1]['created_at']), items[-1]['id']) if items else None
    return {'items': items, 'next_cursor': next_cursor}
files.purpose: Serves keyset pages while still honoring legacy offset during the transition window.

migration_strategy: Ship composite index with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY so no table lock
Deploy endpoint accepting both cursor and offset; clients keep using offset
Release app build that prefers next_cursor when present
Monitor duplicate-card telemetry; expect the 9% repeat rate to fall toward zero on cursor sessions
Once offset traffic drops below 1%, log+deprecate offset param
Remove offset branch after the last supporting app version is below threshold
testing_strategy: Fixture: 10k posts including 50 sharing one created_at timestamp. Assert paging through the entire feed yields each id exactly once (output). Side-effect: assert each returned next_cursor verifies under HMAC and round-trips to the same (ts,id). Chaos test: insert 30 new posts mid-pagination, continue with the held cursor, and assert zero duplicates and zero skips of pre-existing posts; also feed a cursor with a flipped signature byte and assert 400/bad_cursor.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Under concurrent inserts, OFFSET counts shift: a post added at the head pushes every later row down one position, so page N+1 re-returns the last row of page N (duplicate) or skips one, which is exactly the 9% repeat the mobile team sees.

title: Make a NestJS payment-create endpoint idempotent via Idempotency-Key without breaking existing callers
business_context: A fintech POST /payments has no idempotency. On mobile network retries, ~0.3% of payments double-charge, costing ~$40k/month in refunds and support. Some partners already send a Stripe-style Idempotency-Key header that the API currently ignores.
constraints: double-submit within 24h must return the original result, not a new charge
horizontal scale: 8 instances, no sticky sessions
key is optional for old clients (no breaking change)
must survive a crash between charging and responding
Postgres + Redis available; Redis may evict

before_state.description: Each POST creates a new payment; retries create duplicate charges.

issues: No dedupe: a client retry charges the card again
Idempotency-Key header sent by some partners is ignored
No record links a logical request to its charge, so retries can't be matched

reasoning: Naive approach: cache the response in Redis keyed by Idempotency-Key — rejected because Redis can evict the key, and a crash between charge and cache-write still double-charges. Rejected alternative: a unique index only on the key column — rejected because it dedupes the insert but doesn't return the original response body and races on concurrent retries. Chosen: a durable idempotency record with a UNIQUE(key) row inserted in the SAME transaction boundary as the charge intent, status machine (pending->done), and a Redis short-lock to collapse concurrent duplicates. UI/client sends Idempotency-Key -> interceptor reads it -> service tries to claim the key row (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT) -> if claimed-new it charges and stores the response; if conflict it waits for the in-flight one or returns stored response -> config sets the 24h retention -> tests cover concurrent identical requests -> rollout makes the key optional so old clients are unaffected.

after_state.description: Idempotency keyed by a durable Postgres row plus a Redis lock to collapse concurrent retries; old clients without a key behave as before.

files.purpose: Durable dedupe record that survives crashes and Redis eviction; PRIMARY KEY enforces single claim.
Atomically claims a key and persists the canonical response so any instance can replay it.
Optional key path: claim-or-replay; mismatched body under same key is rejected; no key = legacy behavior.

migration_strategy: Ship table + service with the key path gated behind a feature flag defaulting on but no-op for keyless requests
Enable for internal test partner; verify replays return identical body
Document Idempotency-Key in OpenAPI and recommend partners send it
Ramp: monitor duplicate-charge rate; expect the 0.3% to collapse
Add a nightly job to purge keys older than 24h
If anomalies, disable flag -> falls back to keyless behavior with zero downtime
testing_strategy: Fixture: a CreatePaymentDto for $50. Assert two sequential calls with the same key return the identical {id,status} and the charge gateway is invoked exactly once (side-effect via mock call count). Assert the idempotency_keys row transitions pending->done. Chaos test: fire 10 concurrent identical requests across simulated instances sharing the DB; assert exactly one charge and nine replays. Negative: same key with a different amount returns 409 idempotency_key_reuse.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A Redis-only cache loses the dedupe record on eviction or on a crash between charge and cache-write; the next retry finds no key and charges the card again — the exact double-charge boundary causing the $40k/month refunds.

title: Negotiate API version via Accept header media type for a Django healthcare API
business_context: A healthcare records API exposes /patients/{id}. EHR vendors integrate over years and cannot change URLs easily. Compliance requires we never silently change a field's meaning. We need to evolve the `name` field from a string to a structured object without URL churn, serving 220 vendor integrations.
constraints: no URL changes (vendors pin full URLs in signed configs)
default version when no Accept version given must stay v1 forever
HIPAA: audit which vendor requested which version
p99 under 200ms; 500 req/s
one Django service, gunicorn 12 workers

before_state.description: Single serializer returns name as a string; changing it would break vendors who parse it as text.

issues: name is a flattened string; can't carry given/family/prefix structure
No version negotiation; any change is global and breaking
No audit of which vendor saw which representation

reasoning: Naive approach: add /v2/patients URL — rejected because vendors pin full URLs in signed compliance configs and re-signing 220 of them takes quarters. Rejected alternative: a ?version= query param — rejected because it's easy to omit and caches treat it as a separate resource inconsistently, risking stale PHI. Chosen: content negotiation via Accept: application/vnd.ehr.v2+json. UI/client sets Accept -> a versioning class parses the media type into request.version -> view selects PatientSerializerV1/V2 -> data is unchanged (model has structured fields already) -> config registers vendor->version in an audit log -> tests assert default stays v1 -> rollout: vendors opt in by changing only their Accept header.

after_state.description: Accept-header media-type versioning selects a serializer; v1 default frozen; per-vendor version audited.

files.purpose: Parses the vendor's Accept media type into a version, defaulting to frozen v1.
Two serializers keep v1's string name frozen while v2 returns structured name.
Selects serializer by negotiated version and writes the HIPAA version-access audit.
Wires the media-type versioning class globally.

migration_strategy: Deploy v2 serializer + versioning class; default remains v1 so no vendor sees a change
Publish the application/vnd.ehr.v2+json media type in the integration guide
Enable opt-in for one pilot EHR vendor; verify structured name in audit log
Ramp vendors as they update their Accept header; track version mix in the audit table
Keep v1 indefinitely (compliance) but report quarterly on remaining v1 share
Only retire a serializer field after a formal deprecation notice; never silently
testing_strategy: Fixture: a patient with first_name='Ada', last_name='Lovelace', prefix='Dr'. Assert no Accept header -> name is 'Ada Lovelace' (v1 frozen). Assert Accept: application/vnd.ehr.v2+json -> name={'given':'Ada','family':'Lovelace','prefix':'Dr'}. Side-effect: assert record_version is called with (vendor_id, 'v2'). Chaos test: malformed Accept 'application/vnd.ehr.v9+json' falls back to v1, never 500, and audits v1.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A new /v2 URL requires re-signing 220 compliance configs; until then vendors stay on v1 and get zero benefit, and any in-place change to the v1 string would corrupt how EHRs parse patient names — a PHI-integrity incident.

title: Deprecate a Go gRPC field with reserved tags and a oneof migration path
business_context: A gaming backend's gRPC PlayerProfile has a deprecated `int32 level` that should become a richer `Progression` message (level + xp + prestige). 40+ client builds across consoles are in the wild; protobuf wire-compat must hold so old clients keep reading `level` while new clients use `progression`.
constraints: wire-compatible: old clients must still decode the message
never reuse a freed field number
servers and clients deploy independently over months
stream throughput 80k msgs/s; no extra allocations on hot path
field deprecation must be discoverable in generated docs

before_state.description: `level` is a bare int32 with no room to grow; replacing it risks breaking wire compat.

issues: level cannot express xp/prestige; product needs richer progression
No deprecation marker; clients don't know to migrate
Changing field 3's type would break wire compat for shipped console builds

reasoning: Naive approach: change field 3 from int32 to a Progression message — rejected because the wire type changes (varint vs length-delimited) and shipped console clients would fail to decode. Rejected alternative: drop `level` and add `progression` reusing number 3 — rejected because reusing a field number corrupts old clients still reading tag 3 as varint. Chosen: keep `level` as deprecated, add `progression` at a NEW field number, dual-write on the server, and reserve nothing yet. proto adds [deprecated=true] -> server populates both level and progression.level -> new clients read progression -> old clients keep reading level -> config gates when server stops populating level -> tests decode an old-format message with the new server -> rollout retires level only after telemetry shows no old clients.

after_state.description: `level` deprecated but still populated; `progression` added at a fresh field number; wire-compatible both ways.

files.purpose: Adds progression at field 4 and marks legacy level deprecated without reusing field numbers.
Dual-writes legacy level and the new progression so both old and new clients work.

files.content:
package server

import "os"

// When EMIT_LEGACY_LEVEL=false (after old clients drain) we stop populating Level.
var EmitLegacyLevel = os.Getenv("EMIT_LEGACY_LEVEL") != "false"
files.purpose: Flag to stop emitting the deprecated field once telemetry shows no old clients.

migration_strategy: Regenerate stubs; server dual-writes level + progression; deploy server first
Ship new client builds reading progression, falling back to level if progression is nil
Track in telemetry the min client version still reading level
When old-client share hits zero, set EMIT_LEGACY_LEVEL=false to stop populating level
Hold one release with level empty but field present (reserve it later)
Only in a future major proto bump: reserve field number 3 and remove the field
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Player{Level:42,XP:9000,Prestige:1}. Assert toProto sets Level==42 AND Progression{Level:42,Xp:9000,Prestige:1}. Wire test: marshal with the new schema, unmarshal with the OLD generated struct (only fields 1-3) and assert Level==42 still decodes (backward compat). Forward test: marshal with old schema, unmarshal with new struct, assert Progression is nil and client falls back to Level. Chaos: set EMIT_LEGACY_LEVEL=false, assert Level==0 but Progression intact and old-struct decode yields default level (documented behavior).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reusing field number 3 for a length-delimited Progression makes old console clients interpret the bytes as a varint level, decoding garbage or erroring on the stream — and since builds are baked into consoles there is no quick client fix.

title: Contract-first OpenAPI generation to stop drift between a Node API and its TS SDK
business_context: A devtools company hand-writes its OpenAPI spec AND its server routes AND its published TypeScript SDK. They drift: 3 production incidents last quarter were SDK/server field mismatches. 5,000 customers depend on the SDK. We want one source of truth driving validation, docs, and the SDK.
constraints: spec is the contract; server must reject requests violating it
SDK must be generated, not hand-written
CI must fail on any spec/implementation drift
backward-compatible spec changes only on minor releases
build adds <90s to CI

before_state.description: Spec, server validation, and SDK are independently authored and drift.

issues: No request validation; bad payloads reach the DB
Field names/types not tied to any spec
Hand-written SDK can diverge from this shape silently
role typed as plain string, not the server's enum
No id/createdAt in the return type even though server returns them
Maintained by hand, drifts from server

reasoning: Naive approach: keep three artifacts but add a code review checklist — rejected because humans miss drift, which is exactly how the 3 incidents happened. Rejected alternative: generate the spec FROM server code annotations — rejected because it makes the implementation the contract, so a refactor silently changes the public API. Chosen: contract-first — openapi.yaml is the source of truth. Spec -> express-openapi-validator enforces requests/responses at runtime -> openapi-typescript-codegen emits the SDK -> redoc renders docs -> CI diffs the committed SDK against a fresh generation and runs a response-validation suite -> rollout publishes SDK from CI only.

after_state.description: openapi.yaml drives runtime validation, the generated SDK, and docs; CI fails on drift.

files.content:
openapi: 3.0.3
info: { title: Users API, version: 1.4.0 }
paths:
  /users:
    post:
      operationId: createUser
      requestBody:
        required: true
        content:
          application/json:
            schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/CreateUser' }
      responses:
        '201':
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: { $ref: '#/components/schemas/User' }
components:
  schemas:
    CreateUser:
      type: object
      required: [email]
      properties:
        email: { type: string, format: email }
        role: { type: string, enum: [member, admin], default: member }
    User:
      type: object
      required: [id, email, role, createdAt]
      properties:
        id: { type: string }
        email: { type: string, format: email }
        role: { type: string, enum: [member, admin] }
        createdAt: { type: string, format: date-time }
files.purpose: Single source of truth: request and response contracts including the role enum.
Enforces the contract at runtime for both requests and responses; invalid I/O is rejected.
Handler trusts validated input and returns the exact response schema.
Generates the SDK from the spec and fails CI if the committed SDK drifts.

migration_strategy: Author openapi.yaml capturing the current real shapes (no behavior change)
Add express-openapi-validator with validateResponses logging-only first to catch existing violations
Switch validation to enforcing once logs are clean
Replace hand-written SDK with generated client; publish from CI
Add ci:drift gate so PRs fail if SDK isn't regenerated
Deprecate the hand-written SDK package version with a pointer to the generated one
testing_strategy: Fixture: POST /users {email:'a@b.com', role:'admin'} -> assert 201 and response matches User schema including createdAt. Side-effect: assert db.users.create called once. Drift test: hand-edit a field name in a route response and assert the response-validation middleware returns 500 with the schema violation. Negative/chaos: POST with role:'superuser' (not in enum) -> assert 400 from the validator before reaching the DB (db.create not called). CI test: mutate openapi.yaml, run ci:drift, assert non-zero exit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With three hand-maintained artifacts, a developer adds `createdAt` to the server response but forgets the SDK type and the spec; SDK users get an `any`-typed field or runtime parsing bugs — the precise SDK/server mismatch that caused the 3 incidents.

title: Add RFC-8288 Link headers and total-count metadata to a paginated Rails-style list
business_context: A B2B SaaS reporting API returns arrays with no pagination metadata. Dashboards must guess if more pages exist by checking array length == limit, which breaks when a page happens to be exactly full. 3,000 tenants; some pull 200k-row reports.
constraints: existing clients expect a bare JSON array body to keep working
add metadata without changing the array body
Link header must follow RFC 8288 (rel=next/prev/last)
total count query must not double the latency
Postgres, single primary

before_state.description: Bare array with no next/last signal; clients infer pages from length, which is unreliable.

issues: No way to know if a next page exists when length==100
No total count for progress bars
Clients hardcode per=100, coupling to server config

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the body in {data, meta} — rejected because every existing client parses a top-level array and would break. Rejected alternative: add a custom X-Has-More boolean — rejected because it's non-standard and clients still can't build prev/last links. Chosen: keep the array body, move pagination into RFC-8288 Link + X-Total-Count headers. UI reads Link rel=next -> API computes links from page/per/total -> data does one windowed query plus a count -> count is cached per filter-hash in Redis for 30s to avoid doubling latency -> tests assert header correctness at boundaries -> rollout is additive so nothing breaks.

after_state.description: Array body preserved; pagination expressed via standard Link and X-Total-Count headers with a cached count.

files.purpose: Builds RFC-8288 compliant Link header values for next/prev/first/last.
Returns the same array body plus standard pagination headers; count cached to keep latency flat.

migration_strategy: Deploy headers additively; body shape unchanged so all clients keep working
Update the OpenAPI to document Link and X-Total-Count
Update first-party dashboards to follow Link rel=next instead of length checks
Add the per param (capped at 500) and document it; default stays 100
Monitor count-cache hit rate; tune TTL if stale totals confuse progress bars
No removal needed; this is purely additive (rollback = stop sending headers)
testing_strategy: Fixture: 250 reports, per=100. Assert page 2 returns 100 rows (array body unchanged) with Link containing rel=next page=3 and rel=last page=3 and X-Total-Count: 250. Side-effect: assert the count is read from Redis on the second request (db.count called once across two requests). Boundary test: exactly 100 rows total -> page 1 has NO rel=next (the bug the length-check had). Chaos: Redis down -> falls back to db.count, still returns correct headers, no 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping the body in {data,meta} breaks every client doing `response.map(...)` on a top-level array; and the length==limit heuristic falsely reports a next page whenever a page is exactly full, sending dashboards into an empty extra fetch.

title: Roll out a breaking auth-scope change behind a per-tenant API version pin
business_context: An e-commerce platform API ties write operations to coarse scopes (write:all). Security wants granular scopes (write:orders, write:inventory). 1,100 connected apps hold tokens with the old scope. We must enforce granular scopes for new integrations while honoring existing tokens until they re-consent.
constraints: existing tokens with write:all keep working until expiry/re-consent
new app registrations get granular scopes only
scope check must be O(1) per request at 5k req/s
version pin is per-tenant, set at app registration
auditable: log when a legacy scope grants access

before_state.description: A single coarse scope guards all writes; can't tighten without breaking issued tokens.

issues: Hardcoded write:all; granular scopes impossible
Tightening the check would 403 all 1,100 existing apps
No record of legacy-scope usage for migration planning

reasoning: Naive approach: replace write:all with granular checks immediately — rejected because it 403s every issued token instantly. Rejected alternative: accept write:all forever as a superscope — rejected because it never lets security retire the coarse grant. Chosen: a per-app api_version pin ('2024-01' legacy vs '2026-06' granular) decides which scope model applies, with write:all still satisfying granular checks but logged. Client token -> middleware reads the app's pinned version from token claims -> for legacy version, write:all satisfies any write scope (audited); for granular version, the exact scope is required -> config defines the scope->superscope map -> tests cover both pins -> rollout: new registrations pin granular, legacy apps migrate on re-consent.

after_state.description: Scope enforcement is version-pinned per app; legacy write:all still works but is audited, granular apps require exact scopes.

files.purpose: Version-pinned enforcement: granular by default, legacy write:all honored and audited.
Routes now declare the granular scope they require.
New registrations pin the granular version and only allow granular scopes.

migration_strategy: Deploy version-pinned middleware; all existing apps remain pinned 2024-01 (write:all honored)
Pin all NEW app registrations to 2026-06 granular scopes
Email legacy app owners to re-consent for granular scopes
On re-consent, flip that app's api_version to 2026-06
Watch audit.legacyScopeGrant volume trend toward zero
When legacy grants hit zero, remove the LEGACY branch and the write:all path
testing_strategy: Fixture: token A {api_version:'2024-01', scopes:['write:all']} and token B {api_version:'2026-06', scopes:['write:orders']}. Assert A can POST /orders (legacy path) and audit.legacyScopeGrant is called with (app_id,'write:orders'). Assert B can POST /orders without audit. Negative: token C {api_version:'2026-06', scopes:['write:all']} is 403 (write:all no longer a superscope for granular apps). Chaos: token missing api_version claim -> treated as non-legacy -> 403, never crash.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Swapping write:all for granular checks immediately makes every one of the 1,100 connected apps receive 403 on their next write, halting order creation platform-wide until each app re-consents — an outage measured in lost GMV.

title: Version a Kafka event schema with a Schema Registry compatibility gate
business_context: An IoT platform publishes telemetry events to Kafka consumed by 14 downstream services. A team renamed `temp` to `temperature_c` in the producer and broke 9 consumers in prod. We need schema versioning with enforced backward compatibility so producers can't ship breaking event changes.
constraints: consumers deploy independently and lag up to 2h behind
schema changes must be backward-compatible (old consumers read new events)
Avro on Confluent Schema Registry
60k events/s; no per-event registry round trip on hot path
CI must reject incompatible schema before merge

before_state.description: Producer changes the field name freely; consumers break because nothing enforces compatibility.

issues: Field was renamed from temp -> temperature_c with no alias; old consumers can't find it
No compatibility check gates the schema change
Consumers hardcode field names against an unversioned schema

files.content:
const event = { device_id: id, temperature_c: reading }; // renamed, broke consumers
producer.send({ topic: 'telemetry', value: serialize(event) });
issues: Serializes with the new schema without registering a compatible version
No subject-level compatibility setting
No fallback alias for the old field name

reasoning: Naive approach: coordinate a big-bang where all consumers update field names at once — rejected because consumers deploy independently and lag, so there's no atomic moment. Rejected alternative: dual-publish to two topics (telemetry, telemetry_v2) — rejected because it doubles partitions/retention and consumers must be re-pointed anyway. Chosen: evolve the schema with an Avro alias and set subject compatibility to BACKWARD so the registry rejects breaking changes. Producer serializes with the registered schema id -> registry enforces BACKWARD on register -> the new field carries an alias 'temp' so old consumers resolve it -> CI runs a compatibility check before merge -> hot path caches the schema id -> rollout: register schema, then ship producer, consumers migrate lazily.

after_state.description: Schema carries an alias and the subject enforces BACKWARD compatibility; CI blocks breaking changes.

files.purpose: Alias lets consumers still reading 'temp' resolve the renamed field; default keeps it backward-compatible.

files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
SUBJECT=telemetry-value
curl -sf -X PUT "$SR_URL/config/$SUBJECT" -d '{"compatibility":"BACKWARD"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
RESP=$(curl -sf -X POST "$SR_URL/compatibility/subjects/$SUBJECT/versions/latest" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/vnd.schemaregistry.v1+json' \
  --data-binary @<(jq -Rs '{schema: .}' schemas/telemetry.avsc))
echo "$RESP" | jq -e '.is_compatible == true' >/dev/null || { echo 'INCOMPATIBLE schema'; exit 1; }
files.purpose: CI gate: rejects any schema change that isn't BACKWARD-compatible with the registered latest.
Encodes with the registered schema id (cached) so events carry a versioned, registry-validated schema.

migration_strategy: Set subject compatibility to BACKWARD in the registry
Register the aliased schema as a new version (passes BACKWARD because of alias+default)
Add ci/check-compat.sh to the pipeline so future breaking changes are blocked
Deploy the producer encoding with the registered schema id
Let consumers migrate field reads from temp->temperature_c on their own schedule (alias covers the gap)
After all consumers read temperature_c, a future MAJOR can drop the alias under FORWARD review
testing_strategy: Fixture: encode {device_id:'d1', temperature_c:21.5} with the new schema. Assert an OLD consumer schema (reader with field 'temp') decodes temp==21.5 via the alias (cross-version read). Side-effect: assert producer.send called with a value whose first bytes are the magic byte + cached schemaId. CI test: feed a schema that renames temperature_c->t with no alias and assert check-compat.sh exits non-zero. Chaos: registry unreachable at init -> publish throws fast and producer health check fails (no silent bad encoding).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Renaming a field without an alias produces an Avro schema that's NOT backward-compatible: lagging consumers using the old reader schema can't resolve the field and either get the default or fail deserialization — exactly the 9 broken consumers, and nothing stopped the merge.

title: Sunset a deprecated GraphQL field with @deprecated and usage-driven removal
business_context: A B2B GraphQL API exposes User.fullName, but the schema now has firstName/lastName. fullName is computed and used by an unknown set of the 800 API clients. We need to deprecate and eventually remove it without guessing who still queries it.
constraints: deprecation must be visible in schema introspection and tooling
removal only after measured zero usage for 30 days
field-level usage tracking per client app
Apollo Server; 2k req/s
no breaking change to other fields during the process

before_state.description: fullName is a normal field; no deprecation marker and no per-field usage data, so removal is blind.

issues: fullName not marked deprecated; clients have no signal
No telemetry on who queries fullName
Removing it blindly would break unknown clients
Resolver does the concatenation with no usage instrumentation
No way to tell when fullName usage hits zero
No client attribution

reasoning: Naive approach: just delete fullName — rejected because an unknown subset of 800 clients query it and would break. Rejected alternative: keep it forever — rejected because the duplicated computed field is tech debt and confuses new integrators. Chosen: mark @deprecated with a reason, add a field-resolver usage counter keyed by client id, and remove only after 30 days of zero usage. Query asks fullName -> resolver increments a per-client field-usage metric -> schema directive surfaces the deprecation in introspection/IDE -> config holds the planned removal date -> tests assert deprecation is introspectable AND usage is counted -> rollout removes the field only when the metric is flat at zero.

after_state.description: fullName carries @deprecated, every access is attributed per client, and removal is gated on measured zero usage.

files.purpose: Marks fullName deprecated with a concrete removal date visible in introspection and editor tooling.
Counts every fullName access by client so removal can be gated on real, attributed usage.
Extracts a client identifier so field usage is attributable per integrator.

migration_strategy: Deploy @deprecated reason + per-client field usage metric (no behavior change)
Surface the deprecation in the public schema/changelog and notify top users from the metric
Reach out to the specific clients still hitting User.fullName
Wait until fieldUsage{field='User.fullName'} is zero for 30 consecutive days
Remove the field and resolver in a minor schema release (additive removal of a deprecated field)
Keep the metric one release to confirm no late stragglers, then drop it
testing_strategy: Fixture: query { user(id:1){ fullName } } with header x-client-id: acme. Assert response fullName=='Ada Lovelace' AND metrics.fieldUsage.inc called with {field:'User.fullName', client:'acme'}. Introspection test: assert the schema reports fullName isDeprecated with the reason string. Negative/chaos: query without a client id -> usage attributed to 'unknown', never crashes; query firstName/lastName only -> fullName resolver NOT invoked (no false usage).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Deleting fullName outright returns GraphQL validation errors to every client whose persisted/ad-hoc query selects it; with 800 clients and no usage data you can't even tell who you broke until tickets arrive.

title: Migrate a Next.js route handler from JSON arrays to envelope responses behind a version header
business_context: A Next.js app's /api/products returns a raw array. The team needs to add pagination, request id, and error envelopes consistently across 40 endpoints, but the storefront and 3 partner integrations consume raw arrays. We standardize on an envelope without breaking current consumers.
constraints: raw-array consumers keep working until they opt in
envelope is opt-in via X-API-Version: 2026-06 header
consistent envelope across all 40 endpoints (shared helper)
Edge runtime compatible (no Node-only APIs)
request id propagated for tracing

before_state.description: Raw array body; no envelope, no request id, inconsistent error shapes across endpoints.

issues: Raw array: can't attach pagination/meta without breaking consumers
Error shape ad-hoc per route
No request id for tracing across services

reasoning: Naive approach: switch every endpoint to {data,meta} now — rejected because storefront + 3 partners parse raw arrays and break. Rejected alternative: a query param ?envelope=1 — rejected because it pollutes cache keys and is easy to forget. Chosen: a shared respond() helper that returns a raw array when no version header is present and an envelope when X-API-Version>=2026-06. Client sets header -> middleware stamps a request id -> handler calls respond(data, {page}) -> helper branches on version -> config defines the cutover version -> tests assert both shapes -> rollout flips consumers by adding the header, one integration at a time.

after_state.description: Shared responder emits raw arrays for legacy callers and envelopes for versioned callers, with a propagated request id.

files.purpose: One Edge-compatible helper produces legacy arrays or versioned envelopes plus consistent errors and request ids.
Uses the shared responder so the same handler serves both legacy and enveloped clients.
Stamps a request id on every API request for end-to-end tracing.

migration_strategy: Add respond/respondError helpers and middleware; default path returns raw arrays (no change)
Migrate all 40 routes to call respond() while still defaulting to legacy shape
Document X-API-Version: 2026-06 envelope in the API reference
Opt in first-party storefront by sending the header in its API client
Onboard the 3 partners one at a time, verifying each parses the envelope
Once all callers send the version header, make the envelope the default and treat omission as legacy-only for named partners
testing_strategy: Fixture: 120 products. Assert GET without header returns a raw array of 50 (legacy). Assert GET with X-API-Version: 2026-06 returns {data:[...50], meta:{page,per,total:120}, requestId}. Side-effect: assert x-request-id header is present and stable when supplied by the caller. Chaos: force listProducts to throw -> assert respondError returns {error:{code:'internal_error'}} with status 500 and a requestId, not a raw 500 page.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Switching all endpoints to {data,meta} at once makes the storefront's `products.map` and the 3 partners' array parsers receive an object, throwing client-side and blanking product listings sitewide.

title: Add request/response validation and version negotiation to a FastAPI public API with Pydantic v2
business_context: A fintech public API accepts loosely-typed JSON and returns whatever the ORM serializes. A field rename in the DB leaked to clients last month. We need typed contracts and a versioning scheme so internal schema changes don't leak, across 90 integration partners.
constraints: DB column renames must never change the public response
two live versions (v1, v2) with different money representation
reject unknown request fields (strict) to catch client bugs early
FastAPI + Pydantic v2

before_state.description: ORM objects are returned directly, coupling the public shape to the DB schema.

files.content:
from fastapi import FastAPI
from .db import session
from .models import Loan

app = FastAPI()

@app.get('/loans/{loan_id}')
async def get_loan(loan_id: int):
    loan = session.get(Loan, loan_id)
    return loan.__dict__  # leaks ORM internals & db column names
issues: Returns ORM __dict__: DB column renames leak to clients
No request model: unknown/extra fields silently accepted
Money returned as a float, losing minor-unit precision

reasoning: Naive approach: add a single Pydantic response model — rejected because product needs two money representations (float in v1, minor-units object in v2) for different partners. Rejected alternative: keep returning ORM dicts but rename columns carefully — rejected because it permanently couples public API to DB and one missed rename leaks again. Chosen: explicit Pydantic v2 response models per version plus a path-prefixed router. Client hits /v1 or /v2 -> request model with extra='forbid' validates input -> service returns a domain object -> version-specific response model serializes money differently -> config sets default/latest -> tests assert column rename doesn't change output -> rollout adds v2 additively.

after_state.description: Typed request/response models decouple public shape from the DB; two versions express money differently; unknown fields rejected.

files.content:
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict, EmailStr

class LoanCreate(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(extra='forbid')  # reject unknown fields
    borrower_email: EmailStr
    principal_cents: int

class LoanV1(BaseModel):
    id: int
    borrower: str
    principal: float  # legacy float dollars

class Money(BaseModel):
    amount_cents: int
    currency: str

class LoanV2(BaseModel):
    id: int
    borrower: str
    principal: Money
files.purpose: Per-version response models and a strict request model decouple the public contract from the DB.

files.content:
from fastapi import APIRouter, FastAPI
from .db import session
from .models import Loan
from .schemas import LoanV1, LoanV2

app = FastAPI()
v1 = APIRouter(prefix='/v1')
v2 = APIRouter(prefix='/v2')

def _load(loan_id: int):
    l = session.get(Loan, loan_id)
    return l

@v1.get('/loans/{loan_id}', response_model=LoanV1)
async def get_loan_v1(loan_id: int):
    l = _load(loan_id)
    return LoanV1(id=l.id, borrower=l.borrower_name, principal=l.principal_cents / 100)

@v2.get('/loans/{loan_id}', response_model=LoanV2)
async def get_loan_v2(loan_id: int):
    l = _load(loan_id)
    return LoanV2(id=l.id, borrower=l.borrower_name, principal={'amount_cents': l.principal_cents, 'currency': l.currency})

app.include_router(v1)
app.include_router(v2)
files.purpose: Path-versioned routers map the same domain object into version-specific response models.

migration_strategy: Introduce response models mirroring the CURRENT public shape under /v1 (no client-visible change)
Verify in staging that ORM column renames no longer alter /v1 output
Add /v2 with the Money representation; publish OpenAPI for both
Migrate internal services and pilot partners to /v2
Set extra='forbid' on request models after a warn-only period to catch client typos
Deprecate /v1 with a Sunset header once partner migration completes
testing_strategy: Fixture: a Loan{borrower_name:'Sam', principal_cents:250000, currency:'USD'}. Assert /v1 returns principal==2500.0 (float) and /v2 returns principal=={amount_cents:250000,currency:'USD'}. Decoupling test: rename the ORM column borrower_name->borrower_full in the model mapping and assert BOTH responses still expose 'borrower' unchanged. Negative: POST a loan with an extra field 'note' -> assert 422 (extra='forbid'). Chaos: principal_cents not divisible by 100 -> v1 float still serializes, v2 stays exact in cents.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Returning ORM __dict__ ties the public JSON keys to DB column names; a routine column rename in a migration instantly changes the response keys for all 90 partners — the leak that already happened once.

title: Introduce ETag/If-Match optimistic concurrency to a PUT endpoint to stop lost updates
business_context: A B2B SaaS lets multiple admins edit a shared org settings document via PUT /orgs/{id}/settings. Two admins editing concurrently silently overwrite each other (last-write-wins), causing ~15 support tickets/month about 'settings reverting'. We need optimistic concurrency without a heavy locking system.
constraints: concurrent PUTs must not silently clobber each other
no pessimistic locks (multi-instance, 6 nodes)
existing clients without If-Match must still work for now
version token must be cheap to compute
Postgres with a row version column

before_state.description: PUT does last-write-wins; concurrent edits lose data with no conflict signal.

issues: No version check: concurrent PUTs overwrite each other
No ETag returned, so clients can't detect staleness
Clients can't express 'only update if unchanged'

reasoning: Naive approach: add a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE around the read-modify-write — rejected because it serializes editors and holds locks across user think-time on 6 nodes. Rejected alternative: a full event-sourced settings model — rejected as massive over-engineering for a settings doc. Chosen: optimistic concurrency with a row version column surfaced as a strong ETag and gated by If-Match. GET returns ETag=version -> client PUTs with If-Match -> API does UPDATE ... WHERE version = :etag RETURNING -> if zero rows, return 412 Precondition Failed -> config keeps If-Match optional during rollout -> tests cover the conflicting double-PUT -> rollout enforces If-Match after clients adopt it.

after_state.description: Row version drives a strong ETag; PUT with If-Match does a conditional update returning 412 on conflict.

files.content: ALTER TABLE org_settings ADD COLUMN version bigint NOT NULL DEFAULT 1;
files.purpose: Monotonic row version used as the ETag and the optimistic-concurrency guard.
Conditional UPDATE WHERE version=If-Match; 412 on conflict; legacy path preserved during rollout.

migration_strategy: Add version column defaulting to 1; deploy GET returning ETag (additive)
Deploy PUT supporting If-Match while keeping the legacy no-If-Match path
Update the web admin client to send If-Match from the last GET's ETag
Surface a 'settings changed, reload' UX on 412 instead of silent overwrite
Monitor 412 rate to confirm conflicts are now caught not lost
Once clients send If-Match, make it required (reject PUT without it as 428 Precondition Required)
testing_strategy: Fixture: settings row at version 5. Assert GET returns ETag "5". Assert PUT with If-Match 5 succeeds and returns ETag "6". Conflict test: two PUTs both with If-Match 5; assert the first succeeds (ETag 6) and the second returns 412 with no data change. Side-effect: assert DB version increments exactly once per successful PUT (no double-bump). Chaos: PUT with If-Match 999 (stale) -> 412; legacy PUT with no If-Match still applies but bumps version so subsequent conditional PUTs behave.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Last-write-wins means when admin A and admin B both load version 5 and save, B's PUT overwrites A's changes with no error; A's edits vanish — the 'settings reverting' tickets — and nothing in the API ever signals the conflict.

title: Add a backward-compatible field expansion (include=) to avoid N+1 client round-trips
business_context: A marketplace API returns orders with just buyer_id; clients then fetch /users/{id} per order, causing N+1 calls and rate-limit pain for 12 high-volume partners pulling 50k orders/day. We want optional resource expansion (?include=buyer,items) without changing the default response.
constraints: default response shape unchanged (no include = same as today)
expansion must not trigger N+1 on the server either
cap expansion depth/size to protect the DB
auth: a partner can only expand resources it may read
Postgres; 800 req/s

before_state.description: Orders return only ids; clients fan out to fetch related resources, causing N+1.

issues: Only ids returned; clients must call /users/{buyer_id} per order
No way to request related data inline
High-volume partners hit rate limits from the fan-out

reasoning: Naive approach: always embed buyer + items — rejected because it bloats the default response and breaks clients that expect a flat shape, plus wastes bandwidth for clients that don't need it. Rejected alternative: a separate /orders/expanded endpoint — rejected because it duplicates routing and partners must switch URLs. Chosen: an additive ?include= parameter with server-side batch loading. Client passes include=buyer,items -> API parses+whitelists the include list -> data layer batch-loads buyers (WHERE id = ANY) and items in two queries (no N+1) -> auth filters to readable resources -> config caps include cardinality -> tests assert default unchanged and no N+1 -> rollout is purely additive.

after_state.description: Optional ?include= expands whitelisted relations via batched queries; default response unchanged.

files.purpose: Whitelists and bounds include params so clients can't request arbitrary or unbounded expansions.
Adds inline expansions via batched, authz-filtered queries while leaving the default body untouched.

migration_strategy: Deploy include parsing + batched loaders; with no include param the response is byte-identical
Document ?include=buyer,items in OpenAPI with the allowed set and limits
Pilot with one high-volume partner; measure their per-order /users calls drop
Add server metrics on expanded-query cost; tune the include cap if needed
Roll out to remaining partners; watch overall request volume fall
Keep default flat shape indefinitely; expansion stays opt-in (rollback = ignore include)
testing_strategy: Fixture: 200 orders across 40 buyers. Assert GET /orders (no include) returns flat objects with only id/buyer_id/total. Assert ?include=buyer returns each order with a buyer object, fetched via exactly ONE users query (assert db.users.findReadableByIds called once with 40 ids) — proving no N+1. Authz test: a buyer the partner can't read comes back as buyer:null. Negative: ?include=secrets -> 400 unsupported include; ?include with 7 values -> 400 too many includes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always embedding buyer/items changes the default JSON, breaking partners that index fixed keys and ballooning payloads; meanwhile a per-order buyer lookup inside the loop would just move the N+1 from client to server, melting the DB at 800 req/s.

title: Standardize error responses on RFC-7807 problem+json across a Spring-style API
business_context: A logistics API returns errors in 6 different ad-hoc shapes depending on the layer (validation, auth, downstream). 200+ integrators write brittle per-endpoint error parsing. A bad-data incident took 4h to diagnose because the error body lacked a stable code. We standardize on RFC 7807 problem+json.
constraints: all error responses share one machine-readable shape
stable error `type`/`code` that integrators can switch on
include a trace id for support correlation
must not change 2xx success bodies
keep HTTP status codes semantically correct

before_state.description: Each layer throws its own error shape; integrators can't parse errors uniformly.

issues: Validation errors are plain text, downstream errors are {err}
No stable error code to switch on
No trace id for correlating with logs

reasoning: Naive approach: add a try/catch in each handler returning a custom JSON — rejected because it's per-handler and drifts again into 6 shapes. Rejected alternative: only fix the worst endpoints — rejected because integrators still face inconsistency elsewhere. Chosen: a centralized error middleware emitting application/problem+json (RFC 7807) with a typed AppError hierarchy. Handler throws a typed AppError -> the error middleware serializes it to problem+json with type/title/status/detail/code/traceId -> config maps internal errors to safe public messages -> tests assert the shape per error class -> rollout is additive (success bodies untouched).

after_state.description: A typed error hierarchy + central middleware emit RFC-7807 problem+json with stable codes and trace ids.

files.purpose: Typed errors carry a stable machine code and the right HTTP status.
Central RFC-7807 serializer: one error shape with stable code and trace id for every failure.
Handlers throw typed errors and delegate serialization; success path is untouched.

migration_strategy: Add error classes + problem middleware mounted last; wrap one endpoint as a pilot
Verify problem+json shape and Content-Type on the pilot, including trace id
Migrate handlers layer by layer to throw typed errors
Publish the error catalog (code -> meaning) in the developer docs
Notify integrators of the new stable error shape and a parsing migration window
Remove legacy ad-hoc error responses once all handlers throw typed errors
testing_strategy: Fixture: POST /shipments with no address. Assert status 400, Content-Type application/problem+json, body has code='validation_error', type URL, and a traceId echoing X-Trace-Id. Downstream test: stub carrier.create to reject; assert 502 with code='carrier_unavailable'. Success test: valid body returns the unchanged 2xx JSON (no envelope). Chaos: throw a raw non-AppError; assert it maps to code='internal_error' 500 WITHOUT leaking the raw message, but still includes a traceId.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-handler error JSON keeps drifting: a new endpoint returns yet another shape, integrators' switch-on-message parsing fails, and with no stable code or trace id the next bad-data incident again takes hours to correlate logs to the client-visible error.

title: Add field-level sparse fieldsets (JSON:API style) to shrink a Vue dashboard's payloads
business_context: A Vue/Nuxt analytics dashboard pulls /accounts which returns 40 fields per row, but the list view only renders 4. Payloads are 1.8MB for 500 accounts, causing slow first paint on mobile. We want sparse fieldsets so clients request only needed fields, without breaking callers that expect all fields.
constraints: omitting fields[] returns the full object (no breaking change)
field whitelist enforced (no arbitrary column access)
reduce payload by >70% for the list view
auth-sensitive fields never returned even if requested without permission
Go + Postgres backend

before_state.description: Always returns all 40 columns; list view downloads 10x the data it shows.

issues: SELECT * returns 40 fields regardless of need
1.8MB payload for a 4-column list view
No field selection; clients can't trim

reasoning: Naive approach: build a dedicated /accounts/summary endpoint with 4 fixed fields — rejected because every new view needs a new endpoint and it fragments the API. Rejected alternative: let clients filter client-side — rejected because the bytes still cross the wire, which is the actual problem. Chosen: sparse fieldsets via fields[accounts]=id,name,plan,status mapped to a column whitelist. Client requests fields -> handler parses+whitelists -> data SELECTs only those columns -> authz strips fields the caller can't see -> config defines default (all) -> tests assert payload size + default behavior -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: Sparse fieldsets select a whitelisted column subset; default returns all; sensitive fields gated by permission.

files.purpose: Parses fields[] into a whitelisted, permission-filtered column list; default is the full set.
Builds the projection from the whitelisted columns and returns only those fields.

migration_strategy: Deploy field parsing with default = all columns (response identical when fields[] omitted)
Document fields[accounts]=... in the API reference with the allowed set
Update the Nuxt list view to request only id,name,plan,status
Measure payload size drop and first-paint improvement on mobile
Apply sparse fields to other heavy list views incrementally
Keep full-object default forever; sparse fieldsets remain opt-in
testing_strategy: Fixture: 500 accounts. Assert GET /accounts (no fields) returns all 6 fields per row (unchanged). Assert fields[accounts]=id,name,plan,status returns only those 4 keys and the encoded body is >70% smaller (assert byte length). Authz test: a non-admin requesting owner_email gets it silently dropped, not a 403 and not the value. Negative: fields[accounts]=password -> 400 unknown field (no SQL injection, only whitelisted columns reach the query).
what_breaks_naive_approach: SELECT * ships all 40 fields every time; a per-view summary endpoint just multiplies endpoints, and client-side filtering still transfers the full 1.8MB — none of these reduce the bytes that slow mobile first paint, and naive string-concatenated column selection without a whitelist would open SQL injection.

title: Version a webhook payload with versioned event envelopes and per-endpoint version pinning
business_context: A payments platform sends webhooks (payment.succeeded) to 3,000 merchant endpoints. Changing the payload shape (nesting amount under a money object) would break merchants parsing the flat field. We need per-endpoint webhook version pinning like Stripe, so each merchant receives the shape they registered for.
constraints: each merchant endpoint pinned to the webhook version it was created with
old endpoints keep receiving the flat shape indefinitely
payload signed; signature must cover the versioned body
at-least-once delivery; version must be deterministic per endpoint
Kafka -> webhook dispatcher; 5k events/s

before_state.description: One payload shape for all merchants; changing it breaks everyone simultaneously.

issues: Single flat payload; can't change amount shape without breaking all merchants
No version recorded per endpoint
Signature recomputed but not tied to a versioned schema

reasoning: Naive approach: change the payload and tell merchants to update — rejected because 3,000 endpoints can't update synchronously and at-least-once retries would deliver the new shape to unprepared merchants. Rejected alternative: a global flag day — rejected because there is no coordinated cutover across thousands of third parties. Chosen: per-endpoint webhook_version pinning with versioned transformers. Endpoint stores webhook_version at creation -> dispatcher picks the transformer for that version -> the versioned body is signed -> config maps version->transformer -> tests assert each pin gets its shape -> rollout: new endpoints default to latest, existing stay pinned; merchants opt-up via dashboard.

after_state.description: Each endpoint is pinned to a webhook version; a transformer registry produces and signs the matching payload.

files.purpose: Maps each webhook version to its payload shape so pinned endpoints get a stable contract.
Selects the pinned version's transformer and signs that exact body, advertising the version in a header.
Pins each endpoint's version at creation; new endpoints default to the latest shape.

migration_strategy: Add webhook_version column; backfill all existing endpoints to '2024-06' (flat)
Deploy transformer registry + version-aware dispatcher (existing endpoints unchanged)
New endpoints default to '2026-06'; document both shapes
Expose a dashboard toggle for merchants to upgrade their endpoint version
Notify merchants of the new shape; track version distribution
Retire '2024-06' transformer only after zero endpoints remain pinned to it
testing_strategy: Fixture: a payment.succeeded event {amount_cents:4200, currency:'USD'} and two endpoints, one pinned 2024-06 and one 2026-06. Assert the 2024-06 endpoint receives {amount:42}, the 2026-06 endpoint receives {data:{amount:{value:4200,unit:'minor'}}}. Side-effect: assert X-Signature verifies against the exact bytes sent per endpoint and X-Webhook-Version header matches the pin. Chaos: an endpoint with an unknown pinned version falls back to 2024-06 (never sends an unsigned or empty body); retry delivers byte-identical signed payload (deterministic).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Changing the global payload nests amount under money; the 3,000 merchants reading body.amount as a number suddenly get undefined, mis-recording payment amounts — and at-least-once retries keep redelivering the new shape to merchants who never opted in.

title: Replace integer auto-increment IDs in API responses with prefixed ULIDs without breaking old links
business_context: A devtools API exposes integer order ids in URLs (/orders/12345). The ids leak business volume (enumeration) and collide across a planned sharded DB. We want opaque prefixed ids (ord_01H...) but thousands of saved links and 200 integrators store the integer ids.
constraints: existing integer-id URLs must keep resolving during the transition
new responses must return the opaque id
ids globally unique across future shards
no full-table downtime for backfill of 80M rows
Postgres now, sharding later

before_state.description: Integer ids in responses and URLs leak volume and won't survive sharding.

issues: Integer id enumerable: /orders/12345 -> /orders/12346 leaks volume
Auto-increment collides across future shards
URLs and integrator records pin the integer id

reasoning: Naive approach: swap the column to a ULID and update responses — rejected because every saved link and the 200 integrators' stored integer ids break instantly. Rejected alternative: expose ULID only for new orders, integers for old — rejected because clients then handle two id formats forever with ambiguous routing. Chosen: add a public_id ULID column, resolve BOTH formats on read, return public_id in responses, and backfill in batches. Client may pass integer or ulid -> resolver detects format and looks up accordingly -> responses always return public_id -> backfill job populates public_id online -> config flips response id once backfill completes -> tests assert dual resolution -> rollout deprecates integer URLs later.

after_state.description: A ULID public_id is added and backfilled; reads resolve integer or ULID; responses return the opaque id.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN public_id text;
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_public_id ON orders (public_id);
files.purpose: Adds the opaque id column with a unique index built without locking the 80M-row table.
Generates and recognizes prefixed ULIDs so the resolver can branch on format.
Resolves both id formats for backward compatibility while always returning the opaque public_id.
Backfills public_id in 1000-row batches so 80M rows migrate online without locking.

migration_strategy: Add nullable public_id + concurrent unique index; deploy code that writes public_id for new orders
Run the batched backfill job during low traffic until public_id IS NULL count hits zero
Set public_id NOT NULL after backfill completes
Switch responses to return public_id while still resolving integer ids on read
Deprecate integer-id URLs with a Sunset date in docs; notify integrators
After sunset and zero integer-id traffic, drop the integer resolution path
testing_strategy: Fixture: an order with id=12345 and public_id='ord_01H...'. Assert GET /orders/12345 (legacy) and GET /orders/ord_01H... (new) both resolve the same order and BOTH return id='ord_01H...' (opaque). Side-effect: assert POST /orders writes a public_id and the unique index rejects a duplicate. Backfill test: run on 5k seeded rows with null public_id and assert all become non-null and unique. Chaos: malformed id 'ord_xxx' -> findByPublicId returns null -> 404, never a 500 or integer-parse NaN lookup.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Swapping the primary id to a ULID in one step makes /orders/12345 return 404 for every saved link and breaks the 200 integrators storing integer ids; and an in-place column type change locks the 80M-row table, causing an outage.

title: Add rate-limit headers and 429 Retry-After to a Redis-backed API gateway without changing success paths
business_context: A B2B SaaS API has token-bucket rate limiting but returns a bare 429 with no headers, so integrators retry aggressively and get stuck in backoff storms. Support sees 30 tickets/month from partners who can't tell their remaining quota. We add standard rate-limit headers and Retry-After.
constraints: expose remaining/limit/reset without an extra Redis round trip
429 must include Retry-After so clients back off correctly
limiter is per-API-key across 6 gateway nodes (shared Redis)
success responses must not change semantically
atomic decrement to avoid over-admitting at the boundary

before_state.description: Limiter returns 429 with no guidance; clients can't see quota and retry blindly.

issues: Two commands (incr+expire) race: expire may be skipped if incr returns >1 after key TTL reset
Returns only a boolean; no remaining/reset to surface
Caller sends bare 429 with no Retry-After

reasoning: Naive approach: do an extra Redis GET to compute remaining for headers — rejected because it adds a round trip per request at peak. Rejected alternative: in-memory per-node counters — rejected because 6 nodes would each allow the full quota, multiplying the real limit by 6. Chosen: a single atomic Lua script that increments, sets TTL on first hit, and returns count+ttl in one round trip; middleware turns that into standard headers and a 429 with Retry-After. Client request -> middleware runs the Lua INCR+TTL -> computes remaining/reset -> sets RateLimit-* headers on every response -> on overflow returns 429 + Retry-After -> config holds per-plan limits -> tests assert boundary atomicity -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: One atomic Lua call yields count+ttl; middleware emits RateLimit headers on all responses and Retry-After on 429.

files.purpose: Single atomic round trip returns count+TTL, eliminating the incr/expire race and the extra GET.
Emits standard RateLimit headers on every response and a 429 with Retry-After when exhausted.

migration_strategy: Replace the two-command limiter with the atomic Lua version (behavior-equivalent on allow/deny)
Add RateLimit-* headers to all responses (additive)
Add Retry-After to 429s and document the backoff contract
Update the official SDK to honor Retry-After/RateLimit-Reset
Monitor 429 volume and partner retry patterns; expect backoff storms to subside
No removal needed; headers are additive (rollback = stop emitting headers)
testing_strategy: Fixture: limit 5/60s for key K. Assert requests 1-5 return 200 with RateLimit-Remaining decreasing 4..0 and RateLimit-Reset ~60. Assert request 6 returns 429 with Retry-After present and >0. Atomicity test: fire 20 concurrent requests against the shared Redis and assert exactly 5 are admitted (no over-admit from the old incr/expire race). Chaos: simulate the key TTL expiring mid-burst and assert EXPIRE is reset (count restarts), and Redis eval error -> fail-open with a logged warning, not a 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The separate INCR then EXPIRE can drop the TTL if the key is touched right as it expires, leaving a counter that never resets (clients permanently 429'd); and per-node in-memory counters let all 6 gateways each admit the full quota, so the effective limit is 6x — both invisible because the bare 429 carries no quota info.

title: Introduce a major-version API surface with a compatibility shim translating v1 calls to v2 internally
business_context: A healthcare scheduling API has accreted inconsistencies (snake_case + camelCase mixed, booleans as 'Y'/'N'). A clean v2 is needed, but 150 clinics integrate against v1. We build v2 as the real implementation and keep v1 as a thin translation shim so we maintain one core, not two.
constraints: v1 responses byte-stable for existing clinics
one core implementation (v2); v1 is translation only
translation must not lose data (lossless round-trip where possible)
auth and audit shared across both surfaces
p95 < 180ms including translation; 600 req/s

before_state.description: v1 logic is the implementation; building v2 separately would mean two divergent cores.

issues: Y/N booleans and snake_case baked into the core handler
Building v2 separately would duplicate scheduling logic
Mixed conventions make the API hard to document

reasoning: Naive approach: keep v1, write a parallel v2 handler with its own scheduling logic — rejected because two cores drift and double the maintenance/bug surface for clinical scheduling. Rejected alternative: only clean up v1 in place — rejected because changing v1 field shapes breaks the 150 clinics. Chosen: make v2 the single core service with clean types, and implement v1 as a request/response translation shim calling v2. v1 client -> v1 shim maps Y/N+snake_case to the v2 request -> v2 core handles scheduling -> v1 shim maps v2 response back to the legacy shape -> shared auth/audit wrap both -> config routes prefixes -> tests assert lossless translation -> rollout migrates clinics to v2 directly.

after_state.description: v2 is the only implementation; v1 is a thin lossless translation shim over the v2 service.

files.purpose: The clean v2 core: one implementation of scheduling with consistent camelCase + real booleans.
v2 surface is a thin pass-through to the core with clean types.
v1 stays byte-stable by translating to/from the core; no scheduling logic duplicated.

migration_strategy: Extract scheduling into the core module behind the existing v1 handler (refactor, no behavior change)
Add the v1 shim calling the core; assert v1 responses are byte-identical to before
Add the v2 surface and publish its OpenAPI
Migrate pilot clinics to v2; verify clinical workflows
Ramp clinics off v1; emit Deprecation/Sunset on v1
Once v1 traffic is zero, delete the v1 shim; the core is untouched
testing_strategy: Fixture: same logical appointment created via v1 (telehealth='Y') and v2 (telehealth:true). Assert both create one DB row with is_telehealth=true and the SAME start_time. Assert v1 response is {appointment_id, telehealth:'Y', start_time} (legacy shape) and v2 is {appointmentId, telehealth:true, startTime}. Round-trip test: a v1 request's data survives translation to core and back unchanged. Chaos: v1 sends telehealth='Maybe' -> treated as false (==='Y' check), documented; core never receives an invalid boolean.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Two parallel cores drift: a scheduling bug fixed in v2 silently persists in v1 (or vice versa), and for clinical scheduling that divergence is a patient-safety/audit problem — exactly why a single core with a translation shim is required.

title: Make a bulk-create endpoint partially-successful with per-item results instead of all-or-nothing
business_context: A logistics API's POST /shipments/bulk accepts up to 500 shipments but fails the entire batch if one item is invalid, forcing clients to binary-search the bad row. A 3PL partner reports they retry whole 500-item batches 8x/day because of single bad rows. We need per-item results.
constraints: valid items must persist even if some items fail
response reports per-item status with stable index mapping
bulk insert must stay efficient (no 500 individual round trips)
idempotent per item (client_ref dedupe)
Postgres; batch up to 500 items

before_state.description: One invalid item rolls back the whole batch; clients can't tell which item failed.

issues: Sequential creates: first bad item aborts the request, losing prior inserts' transaction or leaving partial state
No per-item status; client can't locate the failure
500 round trips: slow for large batches

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap all inserts in one transaction so it's atomic — rejected because all-or-nothing is the exact pain; one bad row discards 499 good ones. Rejected alternative: insert items one-by-one in autocommit and stop on first error — rejected because it's slow and leaves the rest unprocessed. Chosen: validate all items first, bulk-insert the valid ones via a single multi-row INSERT with ON CONFLICT(client_ref) for idempotency, and return a per-item result array preserving input order. Client posts batch -> validate each item collecting errors -> bulk INSERT valid items in one statement -> build results aligned to input index -> respond 207-style with statuses -> config caps batch size -> tests assert partial success -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: Items are validated, valid ones bulk-inserted idempotently, and a per-item result array preserves order.

files.purpose: Pure per-item validation so the handler can separate valid from invalid before any DB work.
Persists valid items in one bulk insert with idempotent conflict handling and returns ordered per-item statuses.

migration_strategy: Deploy validation + bulk insert + per-item results behind a feature flag for the bulk route
Enable for the 3PL pilot partner; verify partial success and duplicate handling
Document the 207 multi-status response and result schema in OpenAPI
Update SDK helpers to read per-item results and retry only failed indices
Monitor whole-batch retry rate; expect the 8x/day retries to drop
Make the new behavior default; remove the old all-or-nothing path
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5 shipments where index 2 has weight_g=0. Assert response status 207, results[2].status='error' with the weight error, and the other 4 are 'created' AND persisted (assert 4 rows in DB). Idempotency test: resend the same batch; assert previously-created items come back as 'duplicate' (ON CONFLICT) and no new rows. Side-effect: assert bulkInsert is called ONCE (single statement, not per item). Chaos: batch of 501 -> 400 batch_too_large; an item missing client_ref -> 'error', and valid items still persist.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping the batch in one transaction makes a single invalid row roll back all 500, so the partner must binary-search and re-POST whole batches; the sequential loop without a transaction is even worse, leaving an unpredictable partial state with no per-item report.

title: Add long-polling-safe pagination consistency with a snapshot token for an export API
business_context: A fintech export API paginates a transactions report. Because new transactions arrive constantly, a multi-page export double-counts or misses rows, producing reports that don't reconcile. Auditors flagged a $0 discrepancy that was actually a paging artifact. We need point-in-time consistent pagination.
constraints: a single export must reflect one consistent snapshot regardless of concurrent writes
snapshot must not hold a DB transaction open across many client requests
pages must be resumable for up to 1 hour
Postgres; reports can span 2M rows
must work across 4 API nodes (no node affinity)

before_state.description: Each page is a fresh query, so concurrent inserts shift the result set between pages.

issues: No snapshot: rows inserted between pages shift offsets, double-counting/skipping
OFFSET on 2M rows is slow at deep pages
No resumable token; a dropped page restarts the export

reasoning: Naive approach: hold a REPEATABLE READ transaction open for the whole export — rejected because it pins a DB snapshot/connection for up to an hour across 4 nodes, exhausting the pool and bloating WAL. Rejected alternative: copy the whole report to a temp table per export — rejected because 2M rows per export is expensive and doesn't resume across nodes. Chosen: capture a snapshot boundary (max id at export start) in a signed snapshot token, then keyset-paginate WHERE id <= snapshot_max AND id > cursor. Client starts export -> server returns snapshot token (max_id + created_at) -> each page filters to id<=max_id and keysets forward -> token is signed+resumable for 1h -> config holds token TTL -> tests assert no drift under concurrent inserts -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: A signed snapshot token freezes the upper bound; keyset paging within it is consistent, resumable, and node-agnostic.

files.purpose: Signed, TTL-bounded snapshot token carries the frozen upper bound and resume cursor across nodes.
Freezes max id at export start and keyset-paginates within it, returning a resumable next token.

migration_strategy: Add POST to mint a snapshot token (max id captured once)
Add GET that keyset-paginates within the frozen bound and returns next_snapshot
Migrate the export client to start-then-page using the token
Verify exported totals reconcile against a SELECT count WHERE id<=maxId
Deprecate the OFFSET page param with a Sunset date
Remove the OFFSET branch after clients move to snapshot tokens
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5,000 txns; start an export (maxId captured). Insert 200 NEW txns mid-export. Page through to completion and assert exactly 5,000 rows returned, none of the 200 new rows appear, and no id repeats (consistency). Side-effect: assert next_snapshot verifies and advances the cursor monotonically. Resumability: drop a page and resume from the last next_snapshot on a DIFFERENT node; assert continuity. Chaos: tamper a token byte -> 400 bad_token; use a 2h-old token -> 400 token_expired.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fresh per-page queries let concurrent inserts shift OFFSET windows, so a transaction inserted between page 1 and 2 pushes a row across the boundary — double-counted or skipped — producing exports that don't reconcile, exactly the audit discrepancy.

title: Coexist REST and gRPC for the same service via a transcoding gateway without a second implementation
