business_context: A gaming platform's matchmaking is exposed only over gRPC, but partner web integrations need REST/JSON. The team is about to hand-write a parallel REST service. Instead we want gRPC-JSON transcoding so REST and gRPC share one implementation, serving 30 partners.
constraints: one service implementation behind both REST and gRPC
REST paths/verbs must be RESTful (not /Service/Method)
field naming: JSON camelCase, proto snake_case (auto-mapped)
auth via the same interceptor for both surfaces
no per-request reflection cost on the hot path

before_state.description: Only gRPC exists; a hand-written REST service would duplicate matchmaking logic.

issues: No REST surface; partners can't use JSON over HTTP
A parallel REST service would duplicate matchmaking logic
No HTTP mapping annotations on the RPC

reasoning: Naive approach: hand-write an Express REST service that re-implements matchmaking — rejected because it duplicates complex pairing logic and will drift from gRPC. Rejected alternative: put a generic reverse proxy in front translating ad-hoc — rejected because it can't map JSON<->proto fields or RESTful paths reliably. Chosen: annotate the proto with google.api.http and run grpc-gateway (transcoding) so REST requests are translated to gRPC calls into the single server. REST client -> gateway transcodes JSON->proto using http annotations -> same gRPC server + auth interceptor handle it -> proto->JSON back -> config maps paths -> tests assert REST and gRPC hit the same handler -> rollout adds the gateway alongside gRPC.

after_state.description: HTTP annotations + grpc-gateway expose RESTful JSON over the single gRPC implementation with shared auth.

files.purpose: http annotation maps the RPC to a RESTful POST /v1/matches with automatic JSON<->proto field mapping.
Runs the transcoding gateway that forwards REST calls to the single gRPC server.
One auth interceptor on the gRPC server covers both REST (through the gateway) and native gRPC.

migration_strategy: Add http annotations and regenerate gateway stubs (gRPC unchanged)
Deploy the gateway alongside the gRPC server; gRPC clients unaffected
Publish the REST OpenAPI generated from the annotations
Onboard a pilot web partner to POST /v1/matches
Verify REST and gRPC requests hit the same server metrics/logs (one impl)
Scale the gateway independently; keep gRPC for low-latency game clients
testing_strategy: Fixture: a FindMatch handler returning match_id='m1', players=['p1','p2']. Assert a native gRPC FindMatch and a REST POST /v1/matches {"playerId":"p1","mode":"ranked"} BOTH invoke the same server handler (assert handler call count via a spy) and return equivalent data (JSON camelCase matchId vs proto match_id). Auth test: REST call without Authorization -> 401 (mapped from Unauthenticated). Chaos: malformed JSON body -> gateway returns 400 without reaching the handler; gRPC server down -> gateway returns 503, not a hang.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A hand-written REST service re-implements matchmaking; the first time pairing rules change in gRPC but not REST (or vice versa), partners on different surfaces get different match results — a correctness split that's invisible until players complain.

title: Add conditional GET (Last-Modified/If-Modified-Since) to a high-traffic catalog endpoint
business_context: A retail API's GET /catalog/{id} is hit 20k req/s by edge caches and mobile apps, but it always returns 200 with the full body even when nothing changed, burning bandwidth and DB reads. We add conditional GET so unchanged resources return 304, cutting payload and DB load.
constraints: unchanged resources must return 304 with no body
validator must be cheap (no full row read to compute it)
correct under concurrent updates (no stale 304)
CDN-friendly (standard ETag/Last-Modified)
Postgres + Redis; 20k req/s

before_state.description: Always returns 200 + full body regardless of client cache state.

issues: No ETag/Last-Modified: clients always download the full body
Reads the full row even when client has a fresh copy
20k req/s of unchanged catalog data wastes bandwidth and DB

reasoning: Naive approach: compute an ETag by hashing the full JSON each request — rejected because it still reads the full row, defeating the DB-load goal. Rejected alternative: a fixed cache TTL with no validation — rejected because catalog edits must be visible promptly (stale prices). Chosen: store updated_at and serve a cheap validator from a Redis-cached (id -> updated_at) map; on If-Modified-Since/If-None-Match, compare against the lightweight validator and return 304 without the full read. Client sends If-None-Match -> handler fetches just updated_at (from Redis, fallback DB) -> if matches, 304 no body -> else full read + 200 with ETag/Last-Modified -> config caches validators -> invalidation on update -> tests assert 304 path -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: A cheap Redis-cached updated_at validator powers conditional GET; unchanged resources return 304 without a full read.

files.purpose: Cheap validator: returns only updated_at (cached) so conditional checks avoid full-row reads.
Returns 304 from the cheap validator when fresh; only does a full read + 200 when changed.

migration_strategy: Ensure catalog has updated_at maintained on every write; add invalidate(id) to write paths
Deploy validator + conditional GET; clients without conditional headers still get 200 (additive)
Set Cache-Control so CDNs revalidate with ETag
Roll out the mobile client to send If-None-Match from cached ETags
Measure 304 ratio and DB read drop at the edge
Tune validator TTL; no removal (rollback = always 200)
testing_strategy: Fixture: a catalog item with updated_at=T. First GET returns 200 with ETag "T". Second GET with If-None-Match "T" returns 304 with empty body and NO full-row read (assert db.catalog.findById not called on the 304 path). Update test: write the item (updated_at=T2, validator invalidated); GET with If-None-Match "T" now returns 200 (not a stale 304). Chaos: Redis miss -> validator falls back to DB for updated_at only; concurrent update during a request -> next request reflects T2 (no stale 304).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hashing the full JSON for an ETag still reads the full row 20k times/s, so it cuts bandwidth but not DB load; a blind TTL cache serves stale prices after a catalog edit — both miss the goal that conditional GET with a cheap validator achieves.

title: Evolve an enum field additively so old clients don't choke on new values
business_context: A fintech API returns a transaction `status` enum {pending, settled, failed}. Product is adding `disputed` and `reversed`. Many of the 400 integrators have strict client-side enum parsers that throw on unknown values, so naively adding members would crash their apps. We need a forward-compatible enum evolution.
constraints: existing clients must not crash on new enum values
new clients must see the precise new statuses
a documented fallback bucket for unknown values
version-pinned clients can opt into the richer enum
OpenAPI must describe both behaviors

before_state.description: A bare enum; adding members breaks strict client parsers.

issues: Emits new enum values (disputed/reversed) to clients that only know 3 statuses
No fallback for unknown values; strict parsers throw
No version pin controlling which clients see new members

reasoning: Naive approach: just start returning the new statuses — rejected because strict client enums throw on unknown values, crashing 400 integrators' apps. Rejected alternative: never add new statuses, overload 'failed' — rejected because it loses real semantic distinctions (disputed vs reversed differ for accounting). Chosen: version-pinned enum projection — legacy clients get new values folded into a documented superset bucket (disputed/reversed -> 'failed' with a status_detail field), while pinned-new clients get the precise value. Client version -> serializer projects status by version -> legacy maps new members to nearest known + adds status_detail -> new returns raw -> OpenAPI documents both -> config holds the mapping -> tests assert legacy never sees unknown -> rollout: clients opt into the new enum by version pin.

after_state.description: A version-aware serializer folds new enum members into a known value for legacy clients while exposing them precisely to pinned clients, with a status_detail escape hatch.

files.purpose: Maps new enum members to a known legacy value while preserving the exact value in status_detail.
Serializes status per client version so legacy clients never receive an unknown enum value.
Documents that legacy status is a closed set with detail, while v2 status includes the new members.

migration_strategy: Add status_detail to all responses carrying the precise value (additive, safe for legacy)
Deploy version-aware projection; legacy clients still see only {pending,settled,failed}
Publish TxnV2 schema with the expanded enum and a version pin to opt in
Migrate integrators who handle the new values to the 2026-06 pin
Monitor that legacy responses never emit disputed/reversed (guard test in CI)
Once most clients are on v2, recommend reading status_detail and consider promoting the enum in a future major
testing_strategy: Fixture: a txn with status='disputed'. Assert legacy serialization returns status='failed', status_detail='disputed' (legacy parser sees a known value). Assert version '2026-06' returns status='disputed', status_detail='disputed'. Guard test: iterate all possible DB statuses through the legacy projection and assert status is always in {pending,settled,failed} (no unknown leaks). Chaos: a brand-new status 'frozen' not in the fold map -> legacy folds to 'failed' (safe default) with detail='frozen', never throws.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Emitting disputed/reversed directly to all clients makes strict enum parsers (e.g. a Swift/Kotlin sealed enum or a Go switch with no default) throw or crash on the unknown case, taking down 400 integrators' apps the moment the first disputed transaction is returned.

title: Add HATEOAS-style action links to a workflow API so clients stop hardcoding state transitions
business_context: A B2B SaaS approval workflow exposes /requests/{id} with a status, and clients hardcode which buttons (approve/reject/recall) to show based on status string matching. New states broke 4 client UIs last quarter. We add server-driven action links so the server owns allowed transitions.
constraints: clients must render available actions from the response, not hardcoded rules
permission-aware: only actions the caller may perform are listed
adding a new state must not require client code changes
links must include method + href
existing flat fields stay for backward compat

before_state.description: Clients infer available actions from status strings; new states silently produce wrong UIs.

issues: No action links; clients hardcode status->buttons logic
Permission not reflected; clients may show actions the user can't perform
A new status requires every client to update its mapping

reasoning: Naive approach: document the status->actions matrix so clients implement it — rejected because it duplicates the transition rules in every client and breaks on new states (the 4 broken UIs). Rejected alternative: a separate /requests/{id}/actions endpoint — rejected because it's a second round trip and can desync from the resource. Chosen: embed permission-aware _links computed server-side from the state machine. Client renders _links -> server derives allowed transitions from the state machine + caller's permissions -> each link has rel/method/href -> flat fields kept -> config holds the transition map -> tests assert links match state+perms -> rollout additive.

after_state.description: The resource embeds permission-aware action links derived from the server's state machine; flat fields preserved.

files.purpose: Derives the permitted action links from the state machine and the caller's permissions in one place.
Returns the resource plus server-computed action links so clients render from data, not rules.

migration_strategy: Add _links additively; existing clients ignore it and keep using flat status
Migrate one client UI to render buttons from _links.actions
Verify a new status added server-side surfaces correct links with zero client change
Roll the link-driven UI to remaining clients
Add CI assertion that every status in MACHINE has defined transitions
Deprecate client-side transition logic in the SDK docs
testing_strategy: Fixture: a pending request owned by user U; caller A has request:approve, caller B is the owner without approve. Assert A sees approve+reject links; B sees only recall. Permission test: a viewer with neither perm sees no action links. State test: set status='rejected' and assert actions=[] (terminal). New-state test: add 'on_hold' to MACHINE and assert a request in that state returns its links without touching the route. Chaos: unknown status in DB -> actions=[] (no crash), flat fields still returned.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With clients hardcoding status->buttons, adding a state like 'on_hold' makes every client fall through its switch and render the wrong actions (or none), and clients may show approve to users who lack the permission — the 4 broken UIs and a potential authz bypass at the button level.

title: Migrate a Mongo-backed API from embedded documents to references behind a stable response contract
business_context: A social API stores each post with a fully embedded author document. Author profile edits now require updating millions of embedded copies, and stale author names show in feeds. We move authors to a referenced collection while keeping the /posts response shape identical for 250 mobile/web clients.
constraints: /posts response shape unchanged (author still embedded in the JSON)
author edits must reflect immediately, no millions-of-docs update
no read N+1 when listing 50 posts
MongoDB; 100M posts
rollout reversible at each step

before_state.description: Author is embedded per post; profile edits require mass updates and go stale.

issues: Editing an author name requires updating every embedded copy across 100M posts
Embedded author goes stale until a mass update runs
Storage bloat from duplicated author docs

reasoning: Naive approach: keep embedding but run a background job to update all posts on author edit — rejected because updating an author's posts across 100M docs is slow and the window of staleness is unacceptable for feeds. Rejected alternative: change the response to return author_id and make clients fetch authors — rejected because it breaks 250 clients expecting an embedded author and adds N+1. Chosen: store author_id reference, $lookup/join at read time, and re-shape the joined author back into the SAME embedded JSON. Client gets identical shape -> read joins author by id (batched for lists) -> author edits touch one author doc -> config gates dual-read during migration -> tests assert shape stability + freshness -> backfill replaces embedded author with author_id.

after_state.description: Posts reference author_id; reads join the live author and re-embed it, so the response shape is unchanged and always fresh.

files.purpose: Joins the live author at read time and re-embeds it, keeping the response shape byte-stable and fresh.
Lists 50 posts with a single $lookup join, avoiding per-post author fetches (no N+1).
Backfills author_id and upserts authors, then drops the embedded copy, batched to avoid load spikes.

migration_strategy: Create authors collection; deploy reads that prefer author_id+lookup but fall back to embedded author if author_id missing
Backfill: upsert authors and set author_id, leaving embedded author temporarily
Verify joined reads produce byte-identical author JSON
Drop the embedded author field in a second backfill pass
Switch author-edit path to update the single author doc only
If issues, re-enable embedded-author fallback (data still present until the drop pass)
testing_strategy: Fixture: a post with author_id pointing to author {name:'Ada', avatar:'a.png'}. Assert GET /posts/:id returns author:{id,name:'Ada',avatar} (same shape as before). Freshness test: update the author's name to 'Ada L.' and assert the next /posts read shows 'Ada L.' with no post update. List test: GET /feed of 50 posts triggers ONE aggregate (assert single DB call, no N+1). Chaos: an author_id pointing to a deleted author -> $unwind drops it; handle with a fallback author object instead of 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Embedding plus a background fan-out update means an author rename must rewrite every one of their posts across 100M docs; until it finishes, feeds show stale names, and switching clients to author_id directly would break 250 apps that read an embedded author object.

title: Add OpenAPI-driven backward-compat CI check to block breaking changes before merge
business_context: A devtools company ships API changes weekly. Twice this quarter a developer removed a response field or tightened a request type, breaking clients in production. We add an automated breaking-change detector that diffs the proposed OpenAPI against the deployed one and fails CI on incompatible changes.
constraints: CI must fail on removed fields, narrowed types, new required request fields
additive changes (new optional fields, new endpoints) must pass
the deployed spec is the baseline (fetched in CI)
must run in <60s
allow explicit, reviewed overrides for intentional breaking changes (major bump)

before_state.description: No automated compatibility gate; breaking spec changes reach production.

files.content:
name: api
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npx @redocly/cli lint openapi.yaml  # lint only, no compat check
issues: Only lints syntax; does not detect breaking changes vs production
A removed response field passes CI
No baseline comparison against the deployed spec

reasoning: Naive approach: manual reviewer checklist for breaking changes — rejected because reviewers miss them (the two incidents). Rejected alternative: semantic-version the spec and trust authors to bump major — rejected because authors forget and there's no enforcement. Chosen: oasdiff-based CI gate that fetches the deployed spec as the baseline and fails on breaking changes unless a labeled major override is present. PR opens -> CI downloads deployed openapi.json -> oasdiff compares -> breaking changes -> fail unless 'breaking-change-approved' label -> config defines the deployed-spec URL -> tests cover a removed field failing -> rollout adds the gate non-blocking first.

after_state.description: CI diffs the PR spec against the deployed baseline with oasdiff and blocks breaking changes unless explicitly approved.

files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
curl -sf "$DEPLOYED_SPEC_URL" -o /tmp/baseline.yaml
# oasdiff exits non-zero when breaking changes are found
if oasdiff breaking /tmp/baseline.yaml openapi.yaml --fail-on ERR; then
  echo 'No breaking changes.'
else
  if [ "${ALLOW_BREAKING:-false}" = 'true' ]; then
    echo 'Breaking changes present but explicitly approved (major bump).'
    exit 0
  fi
  echo 'BREAKING API change detected. Add the breaking-change-approved label for a major version bump.'
  exit 1
fi
files.purpose: Fails CI on breaking spec changes vs the deployed baseline, with an explicit approved-override escape.

files.content:
name: api
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  compat:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm i -g @redocly/cli
      - run: npx @redocly/cli lint openapi.yaml
      - name: install oasdiff
        run: curl -sfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/oasdiff/oasdiff/main/install.sh | sh
      - name: breaking change check
        env:
          DEPLOYED_SPEC_URL: ${{ vars.DEPLOYED_SPEC_URL }}
          ALLOW_BREAKING: ${{ contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'breaking-change-approved') }}
        run: bash scripts/breaking-check.sh
files.purpose: Wires the compatibility gate into PR CI, deriving the override from a PR label.

migration_strategy: Add the compat job as non-blocking (continue-on-error) to surface findings for a week
Review flagged PRs to calibrate false positives
Flip the job to required in branch protection
Document the breaking-change-approved label + major-bump process
Add a release step that publishes the new spec as the next baseline
Track breaking-change incidents; expect them to fall to zero
testing_strategy: Fixture spec baseline with a response field 'createdAt'. Test 1 (additive): PR adds an optional response field -> oasdiff reports no ERR -> CI passes. Test 2 (breaking): PR removes 'createdAt' -> oasdiff exits non-zero -> breaking-check.sh exits 1 (assert non-zero). Override test: same removal with ALLOW_BREAKING=true -> exits 0. Side-effect: assert it fetches the baseline from DEPLOYED_SPEC_URL. Chaos: baseline URL 404 -> script fails fast (set -e), not silently passing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A reviewer checklist relies on humans noticing that a PR removed a response field or made a request field required; under weekly shipping pressure they miss it, the change deploys, and clients parsing the removed field or omitting the now-required field break in production — the two incidents.

title: Replace synchronous long-running POST with 202 Accepted + status polling resource
business_context: A media SaaS POST /renders synchronously processes video for up to 90s, hitting gateway timeouts (30s) and tying up worker threads. 18% of render requests time out client-side though the render actually completes. We move to an async job model returning 202 with a status URL.
constraints: clients must get an immediate response (no 30s+ wait)
existing synchronous clients need a migration path
job status must be pollable and eventually consistent
exactly-once job creation per request (no duplicate renders)
Redis/queue + Postgres; 200 renders/min peak

before_state.description: Synchronous render blocks the request and times out at the gateway.

issues: 90s synchronous work exceeds the 30s gateway timeout
Client times out though the render completes -> 18% false failures
Worker thread tied up for the whole render

reasoning: Naive approach: raise the gateway timeout to 120s — rejected because it ties up connections/threads and just moves the cliff while killing throughput. Rejected alternative: fire-and-forget with no status resource — rejected because clients can't learn the result or handle failures. Chosen: enqueue a job, return 202 + Location of a status resource, process async, poll for completion. Client POSTs -> handler creates a job row (idempotent on request key) + enqueues -> returns 202 with Location: /renders/{jobId} -> worker renders and updates job status -> client polls GET status until done -> config tunes poll hints (Retry-After) -> tests assert 202 + eventual completion -> rollout keeps a sync shim for old clients.

after_state.description: POST enqueues a job and returns 202 + status URL; a worker processes it; clients poll a status resource.

files.purpose: POST returns 202 + Location with idempotent job creation; GET exposes a pollable status resource.
Processes the render off the request path and records terminal status for polling.
Bridges old synchronous clients by polling internally within the timeout, falling back to 202.

migration_strategy: Add job table, queue, worker; deploy POST /renders returning 202 + status resource
Keep /renders-sync shim so existing sync clients keep working under the timeout
Migrate clients to the 202 + poll pattern; document Retry-After
Monitor the 18% client-side timeout rate dropping to ~0
Deprecate /renders-sync with a Sunset date
Remove the sync shim after clients adopt polling
testing_strategy: Fixture: a render input. Assert POST /renders returns 202 with Location:/renders/{id}, status 'queued', and a job row created exactly once for a repeated Idempotency-Key (assert one row, one queue add). Assert worker transitions queued->processing->done and GET status then returns status='done' with url. Failure test: make renderVideo throw; assert status='failed' with error and GET returns 200 status=failed (not a 500 from polling). Chaos: duplicate POST with the same Idempotency-Key returns the same job id, no second render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Raising the gateway timeout to 120s keeps the connection and a worker thread occupied per render; at 200 renders/min the pool saturates and unrelated requests queue, turning a slow endpoint into a site-wide latency incident — and the timeout cliff still exists for renders over 120s.

title: Split a god endpoint into resource-oriented routes while keeping the legacy combined route alive
business_context: An IoT platform has POST /device-action that takes an 'op' field switching between reboot, firmware-update, and config-push with wildly different payloads. The endpoint is 600 lines, hard to authorize, and 80 integrations call it. We split into resource routes while keeping the combined route as a compatibility facade.
constraints: legacy POST /device-action keeps working for 80 integrations
each operation gets its own route with op-specific validation + authz
one core per operation (no logic duplication between facade and new routes)
firmware-update authz differs from reboot (stricter)
reversible per operation

before_state.description: One endpoint multiplexes three operations with mixed validation and coarse authz.

issues: Single coarse authz: firmware-update should require device:firmware not device:write
600-line switch is hard to validate per op
All payloads share one loose body schema

reasoning: Naive approach: keep one endpoint but add per-op authz inside the switch — rejected because the body stays a loose union and the file keeps growing, and validation per op is still tangled. Rejected alternative: delete the combined route and force all 80 integrations to migrate now — rejected because that's a flag-day break. Chosen: extract op cores, add resource routes (/devices/{id}/reboot etc.) with op-specific authz/validation, and turn /device-action into a thin facade dispatching to the same cores. Legacy client -> facade dispatches by op to the core -> new clients call the resource route -> shared cores enforce op authz -> config maps op->route -> tests assert parity -> rollout migrates per op.

after_state.description: Per-operation cores back both new resource routes (with op-specific authz/validation) and a thin legacy facade.

files.purpose: One core per operation with op-specific authz and validation; shared by new routes and the legacy facade.
Resource-oriented routes with correct per-op authz, each delegating to its core.
Legacy facade now dispatches to the same cores, so authz/validation match the new routes exactly.

migration_strategy: Extract op cores with correct per-op authz behind the existing combined route (behavior tightens for firmware only)
Add resource routes delegating to the cores
Verify the legacy facade and new routes produce identical results per op
Migrate integrations operation-by-operation to resource routes
Emit Deprecation on /device-action with a Sunset date
Remove the facade after all 80 integrations move and op traffic on it is zero
testing_strategy: Fixture: a user with device:write but NOT device:firmware. Assert POST /devices/1/reboot succeeds; POST /devices/1/firmware returns 403 (stricter authz now enforced) on BOTH the new route and the legacy facade (op='firmware-update'). Parity test: the same op through facade and resource route returns identical results. Negative: /device-action with op='nuke' -> 400 unknown op; firmware without image -> 400. Chaos: core throws a 500-class error -> mapped to 500 with message, never an unhandled rejection.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The coarse device:write check lets anyone who can reboot also push firmware; keeping the god switch and patching authz inline tends to miss one branch, leaving the firmware path under-protected — a security gap, while the union body schema lets malformed firmware payloads through.

title: Add server-driven pagination defaults and max caps to stop unbounded list queries
business_context: A B2B SaaS GET /invoices allows ?limit with no cap; a buggy client sent limit=1000000 and OOM-killed an API node, causing a 6-minute outage. We need sane defaults, hard caps, and a clear over-limit error, without breaking clients passing reasonable limits.
constraints: hard max limit enforced server-side (cannot be exceeded)
clients passing valid limits keep working unchanged
over-limit returns a clear 400 with the allowed max
default limit applied when none given
Postgres; some tenants have 5M invoices

before_state.description: Unbounded limit lets a single request load millions of rows into memory.

issues: No max cap: limit=1000000 loads millions of rows -> OOM
No validation of negative/NaN limits
Default exists but max is unbounded

reasoning: Naive approach: clamp limit silently to a max — rejected because clients asking for 1000 then receiving 100 silently get incomplete pages and can't tell. Rejected alternative: reject any limit over default — rejected because legitimate bulk readers need larger pages. Chosen: validate limit, apply a default, enforce a hard MAX with a 400 error that states the allowed max so clients self-correct. Client sends limit -> middleware parses+validates -> over MAX returns 400 with allowed max -> within range proceeds -> config holds DEFAULT/MAX per plan -> tests assert cap + error -> rollout warns before enforcing.

after_state.description: Limit is validated, defaulted, and hard-capped with an explicit over-limit error.

files.purpose: Centralizes limit validation, default, and hard cap with a clear error payload.
Applies validated limit so no request can load an unbounded result set.

migration_strategy: Deploy resolveLimit in warn-only mode: log when a request exceeds MAX but still clamp
Review logs to confirm no legitimate client routinely needs >MAX
Flip to enforcing: over-MAX returns 400 with the allowed max
Document DEFAULT/MAX and the error in OpenAPI
Add per-plan MAX if enterprise tenants justify larger pages
Rollback = warn-only mode (single config flag), no data risk
testing_strategy: Fixture: a tenant with 5M invoices. Assert no limit -> 50 rows. Assert limit=500 -> 500 rows. Assert limit=1000000 -> 400 invalid_limit with max:500 and NO DB query executed (assert db.invoices.find not called). Negative: limit=-5 and limit=abc -> 400. Chaos: limit=500 against the 5M-row tenant returns promptly (bounded), proving the OOM path is closed; warn-only mode logs but still serves clamped during rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Silently clamping makes a client requesting limit=1000 think it got everything when it got 500, dropping invoices from reconciliation; leaving it unbounded keeps the OOM crash one bad client away from another node-killing outage.

title: Version a Django REST serializer change using a transformation pipeline keyed by request version
business_context: An e-commerce API's product serializer flattens variant options into a single string 'color:red;size:M'. A new mobile app needs structured options, but the web app parses the string. Rather than fork serializers per change, we build a versioned transformation pipeline so each version is a delta over the base.
constraints: each version is expressed as a transform over the previous (no full re-serialization fork)
base shape stays the source of truth
adding a future version must not touch existing version code
request version from URL prefix

before_state.description: A single serializer hard-codes the flattened string; any change forks the whole serializer.

issues: options is a flattened string; structured form impossible without a fork
Each new shape would copy the whole serializer
No way to express a version as a delta

reasoning: Naive approach: copy ProductSerializer into ProductSerializerV2 — rejected because every shared field is duplicated and the two drift on unrelated changes. Rejected alternative: branch inside get_options on request.version — rejected because the serializer accretes version conditionals and becomes unreadable. Chosen: serialize the BASE (richest) shape once, then apply a registry of version transforms that downgrade toward older shapes. Request version -> base serializer builds the full structured representation -> a transform chain reduces it to the requested version's shape -> config maps version->transform -> tests assert each version output -> adding a version = adding one transform.

after_state.description: A base serializer emits the richest shape; version transforms downgrade it, so versions are deltas not forks.

files.purpose: Single base serializer producing the structured (newest) representation as the source of truth.
Versioned deltas over the base; adding a new version adds one function, never edits existing ones.
Serializes once to base then applies the request version's transform.

migration_strategy: Introduce the base serializer producing the structured form (internal only)
Add to_v1 transform reproducing the exact current flattened string; serve v1 by default
Assert v1 output is byte-identical to the legacy serializer
Add /v2 returning the structured base for the mobile app
Migrate web app to v2 when ready; keep v1 transform indefinitely
Future shapes add a new transform without editing v1/v2
testing_strategy: Fixture: a product with options [{color:red},{size:M}]. Assert v1 returns options=='color:red;size:M' (legacy string, byte-identical). Assert v2 returns options==[{key:'color',value:'red'},{key:'size',value:'M'}]. Regression test: snapshot v1 against the old serializer's output and assert equality. Extensibility test: add a to_v3 that, say, renames key->name and assert v1/v2 outputs are unchanged. Chaos: unknown version -> defaults to base (v2), never 500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Forking the serializer per version duplicates the id/name/price fields; a later fix to price formatting gets applied to v2 but forgotten in the v1 copy, so the two versions silently diverge on a field unrelated to the original change.

title: Introduce per-tenant API deprecation dashboard fed by structured usage logging
business_context: A fintech platform wants to deprecate several v1 endpoints but flies blind on who uses them. Past deprecations slipped because a few big customers were still calling sunset routes. We add structured per-tenant per-endpoint usage logging powering a deprecation-readiness dashboard before any sunset.
constraints: log every request's tenant, route, api_version with low overhead
aggregation must not block the request path
data retained 90 days for migration planning
PII-free logs (tenant id only, no payload)
8 API nodes; logs aggregate centrally

before_state.description: No structured usage data; deprecation decisions are guesses.

issues: console.log lines aren't queryable by tenant/version
Synchronous logging on the hot path adds latency
No retention or aggregation for deprecation planning

reasoning: Naive approach: grep the unstructured logs when planning a sunset — rejected because lines aren't keyed by tenant/version and are unreliable to aggregate. Rejected alternative: write a row to Postgres per request — rejected because synchronous DB writes per request add latency and contention at scale. Chosen: emit a structured event (tenant, route_template, version) to an async buffered sink (Kafka/queue), aggregated into a usage table powering a dashboard. Request -> middleware captures route template + tenant + version -> pushes to an in-memory batch flushed async -> aggregator rolls up counts -> dashboard query reads aggregates -> config sets retention -> tests assert events emitted not blocking -> rollout before any sunset.

after_state.description: Async structured usage events feed an aggregated per-tenant per-endpoint table that drives a deprecation dashboard.

files.purpose: Buffers structured usage events and flushes them async so the request path stays fast.
Captures templated route + tenant + version per request without logging any payload/PII.

files.content:
INSERT INTO api_usage_daily (day, tenant, route, version, hits)
SELECT date_trunc('day', to_timestamp(ts/1000)) AS day, tenant, route, version, count(*)
FROM api_usage_raw
WHERE ts >= extract(epoch from now() - interval '1 day')*1000
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
ON CONFLICT (day, tenant, route, version) DO UPDATE SET hits = excluded.hits;
files.purpose: Daily rollup powering the dashboard query of who still calls each v1 route.

migration_strategy: Deploy async usage tracking middleware (no behavior change, negligible latency)
Stand up the Kafka consumer + raw table + daily rollup
Build the dashboard: per-tenant v1 route hits over time
Before any sunset, require v1 traffic for that route at zero for 30 days
Email the specific tenants still on a route from the dashboard
Sunset only after the dashboard confirms zero usage; keep logging to catch stragglers
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100 requests to /v1/orders from tenant 't1' and 5 to /v2/orders from 't2'. Assert track is called once per finished response with the templated route (/v1/orders, not /v1/orders/123). Side-effect: assert flush batches events to Kafka and clears the buffer; the request path does NOT await the send. Aggregation test: run rollup and assert api_usage_daily has hits=100 for (t1,/v1/orders,v1). Chaos: Kafka down -> flush errors are swallowed/logged, requests still succeed, buffer drains on reconnect.
what_breaks_naive_approach: console.log can't answer 'which tenants still call /v1/orders', so a sunset proceeds and a few large customers calling the route from the dashboard's blind spot get hard failures — exactly the slipped deprecations; and a synchronous per-request DB insert would add measurable latency to every call.

title: Migrate filtering from ad-hoc query params to a typed, validated filter grammar
business_context: A logistics API's GET /shipments accepts arbitrary query params mapped straight into a SQL WHERE, which caused an injection scare and lets clients filter on unindexed columns, producing 8s queries. We introduce a typed filter grammar (field-op-value) that's validated, whitelisted, and index-aware, while supporting common existing filters.
constraints: only whitelisted fields/operators allowed
filters on non-indexed fields rejected or routed to a safe path
existing simple equality filters (?status=x) keep working
no raw string interpolation into SQL
Postgres; 1M shipments per large tenant

before_state.description: Query params are concatenated into SQL, enabling injection and slow scans.

issues: String interpolation: ?status=' OR '1'='1 is SQL injection
Filters on any column, including unindexed -> 8s scans
No type validation; ?weight=abc breaks the query

reasoning: Naive approach: parameterize the same arbitrary params — rejected because it stops injection but still allows unindexed slow filters and untyped values. Rejected alternative: a heavy query language like GraphQL filtering — rejected as over-engineering and still needs the same whitelist. Chosen: a typed filter grammar with a field registry (type + allowed ops + indexed flag) compiled to parameterized SQL. Client sends filter[status]=eq:shipped -> parser validates field/op/type against the registry -> rejects non-indexed unless allowed -> builds a parameterized clause -> config holds the registry -> tests assert injection blocked + index enforcement -> rollout maps legacy equality params into the grammar.

after_state.description: A validated filter grammar compiles whitelisted, typed, index-aware filters into parameterized SQL.

files.purpose: Validates field/op/type against a registry, blocks non-indexed range filters, and emits parameterized SQL.
Applies the compiled, parameterized filter and bridges legacy equality params into the grammar.

migration_strategy: Add the filter registry + compiler; map legacy equality params into it (no client change)
Run in warn mode for filters that would now be rejected, logging offenders
Notify clients using non-indexed range filters to adjust or request an index
Flip to enforcing: invalid/unsafe filters return 400
Add indexes for fields clients legitimately need to range-filter
Remove the legacy raw-where path entirely
testing_strategy: Fixture: shipments with statuses. Assert filter[status]=eq:shipped returns only shipped and uses a parameterized query (assert params=['shipped'], no interpolation). Injection test: filter[status]=eq:' OR '1'='1 -> the value is bound as a literal param, returning zero rows (not all rows). Index test: filter[weight_g]=gt:1000 -> 400 (non-indexed range). Legacy test: ?status=delivered still works. Chaos: filter[weight_g]=eq:abc -> 400 int expected; unknown field -> 400.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Interpolating params into SQL means ?status=' OR '1'='1 returns the whole table (injection/data exposure), and unrestricted columns let a client range-filter an unindexed weight column, triggering 8s sequential scans that pile up and degrade the whole API.

title: Add content negotiation for CSV vs JSON export keeping one handler and contract
business_context: A B2B analytics API's /reports/export returns JSON, but 60% of users immediately convert it to CSV for spreadsheets, often with buggy client-side flatteners. We add Accept-based negotiation so the same endpoint streams CSV or JSON from one handler, without a parallel /export.csv route.
constraints: one endpoint, content negotiated via Accept header
CSV must stream (reports up to 500k rows, no buffering whole file)
JSON behavior unchanged for existing clients
stable column order in CSV
correct escaping of commas/quotes/newlines

before_state.description: JSON-only export; clients flatten to CSV with inconsistent, buggy logic.

issues: Loads all rows into memory; 500k rows OOM risk
No CSV support; clients reinvent flattening with escaping bugs
Single format; spreadsheet users do extra client work

reasoning: Naive approach: add a ?format=csv param building a CSV string in memory — rejected because 500k rows buffered to a string blows memory. Rejected alternative: a separate /reports/export.csv route — rejected because it duplicates query logic and auth. Chosen: Accept-based negotiation with a streaming response; JSON streams an array, CSV streams rows through a proper CSV stringifier with stable columns and correct escaping. Client sets Accept -> handler picks the streamer -> data is read via a cursor -> rows pipe to the chosen encoder -> config defines column order -> tests assert escaping + streaming -> rollout additive (default JSON).

after_state.description: One streaming handler negotiates CSV or JSON via Accept, using a DB cursor and a correct CSV encoder.

files.purpose: Streams rows to RFC-4180 CSV with stable columns and correct quote/comma/newline escaping.
Negotiates format via Accept and streams either CSV or a JSON array from a DB cursor without buffering.

migration_strategy: Convert the export to a streaming cursor (JSON output identical, now memory-safe)
Add CSV streaming behind Accept: text/csv (additive)
Document the negotiation and CSV column contract in OpenAPI
Update the dashboard's 'Export CSV' button to send Accept: text/csv
Deprecate client-side CSV flatteners in SDK docs
Keep JSON as default; rollback = ignore Accept and always JSON
testing_strategy: Fixture: rows including a value with a comma and a quote, e.g. amount note 'a, "b"'. Assert Accept: text/csv returns a CSV with the header line in COLUMNS order and the tricky value correctly quoted/escaped. Assert no Accept / Accept: application/json returns the same JSON as before. Streaming test: a 500k-row report completes without buffering the whole body (assert response starts before all rows are read). Chaos: cursor errors mid-stream -> response is destroyed (no truncated-but-200 body); a value with embedded newline is quoted, not row-split.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Building the CSV/JSON in a single in-memory string for a 500k-row report exhausts heap and OOMs the node; client-side CSV flatteners mis-handle values containing commas or quotes, silently corrupting the spreadsheet (a field with 'a, b' splits into two columns).

title: Introduce request signing (HMAC) to a partner API while grandfathering API-key-only callers
business_context: A fintech partner API authenticates with a static API key in a header. A leaked key let an attacker replay requests for days. We add HMAC request signing (key id + signature + timestamp + nonce) to prevent replay and tampering, while existing API-key-only partners keep working during migration.
constraints: signed requests reject replay (timestamp + nonce) and tampering
unsigned API-key-only requests still work during a grace window
clock skew tolerance of ±5 minutes
nonce store shared across 6 nodes (Redis)
no secret in the request body or URL

before_state.description: Static API key only; a leaked key allows unlimited replay and tampering.

issues: Static key: leak -> indefinite replay
No request integrity; body can be tampered with a valid key
No timestamp/nonce to bound or dedupe requests

reasoning: Naive approach: rotate keys faster — rejected because rotation doesn't stop replay within a key's lifetime and adds ops burden. Rejected alternative: mutual TLS — rejected as too heavy for many small partners to deploy quickly. Chosen: HMAC signing over (method, path, body hash, timestamp, nonce) with a per-partner secret, verified server-side, replay-protected by a Redis nonce set and timestamp window; API-key-only path grandfathered behind a per-partner flag. Client signs -> middleware recomputes HMAC -> checks signature, skew, and nonce-not-seen -> stores nonce -> config holds grace flags -> tests assert replay rejected -> rollout flips partners to required signing.

after_state.description: HMAC signing with timestamp+nonce replay protection; API-key-only grandfathered per partner during migration.

files.purpose: Verifies HMAC over a canonical request, enforces clock skew, and rejects replayed nonces via Redis.
Enforces signing for migrated partners, verifies optional signatures otherwise, grandfathers key-only callers.

migration_strategy: Deploy signing verification; all partners start with require_signing=false (key-only still works)
Publish the signing spec + SDK; partners begin sending signatures (verified if present)
Per partner, flip require_signing=true once their traffic is consistently signed
Monitor invalid_signature and replayed-nonce metrics during each flip
Set a global deadline after which require_signing defaults true for all
Rollback per partner = set require_signing=false (no redeploy)
testing_strategy: Fixture: a partner with a known secret. Assert a correctly signed request passes; a request with a tampered body (changed amount) fails (signature mismatch). Replay test: send a valid signed request twice with the same nonce; assert the second returns 401 invalid_signature (nonce replayed). Skew test: timestamp 10 minutes old -> 401. Grandfather test: a key-only partner (require_signing=false) with no signature still passes. Chaos: Redis down during nonce check -> fail closed (reject) for signing-required partners, logged.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Faster key rotation still leaves a live window where a leaked key replays valid requests and tampers bodies; without a per-request signature, timestamp, and nonce there is no way to detect or stop the replay that ran for days.

title: Add bidirectional cursor pagination (before/after) to a Relay-style GraphQL connection
business_context: A social GraphQL API exposes comments as a plain list; the client needs both forward and backward infinite scroll around a focused comment. The current list returns all comments, causing 3MB responses on viral posts. We implement Relay cursor connections with pageInfo and bidirectional paging.
constraints: support first/after AND last/before
stable cursors under inserts
pageInfo.hasNextPage/hasPreviousPage must be accurate
cap page size at 100
Postgres; some posts have 200k comments

before_state.description: Returns the full comment list; huge payloads and no paging primitives.

issues: Returns all comments: 3MB on viral posts
No cursors; client can't page or focus around a comment
No hasNextPage/hasPreviousPage signals

reasoning: Naive approach: add limit/offset args — rejected because offset is unstable under inserts and can't page backward cleanly around a focal comment. Rejected alternative: forward-only keyset — rejected because the focus-around-a-comment UX needs backward paging too. Chosen: Relay connection with (created_at,id) cursors supporting first/after and last/before, computing hasNextPage by over-fetching one row. Client queries connection with first/after or last/before -> resolver decodes cursor -> keyset query in the right direction (fetch limit+1 to detect more) -> builds edges + pageInfo -> config caps page size -> tests assert bidirectional + pageInfo -> rollout adds the connection alongside the legacy field.

after_state.description: A Relay cursor connection supports bidirectional keyset paging with accurate pageInfo.

files.purpose: Encodes/decodes opaque (created_at,id) cursors used in both paging directions.
Implements bidirectional keyset paging with limit+1 over-fetch to set hasNextPage/hasPreviousPage accurately.

migration_strategy: Add commentsConnection field alongside the legacy comments list
Add a composite index on comments(post_id, created_at, id)
Migrate the client to the connection for forward scroll first
Add backward paging (last/before) for focus-around-a-comment UX
Deprecate the legacy comments field with a schema @deprecated
Remove the legacy field after clients move to the connection
testing_strategy: Fixture: 250 comments. Forward: first:20 -> 20 edges, pageInfo.hasNextPage=true, hasPreviousPage=false. Page with after:endCursor and assert continuity (no dupes). Backward: last:20/before:cursor -> previous 20 in correct order, hasPreviousPage accurate. pageInfo test: at the very end, hasNextPage=false. Side-effect: assert each cursor decodes to a real (created_at,id). Chaos: insert 10 new comments mid-scroll, continue forward, assert no duplicates of already-seen edges; page size 500 requested -> capped to 100.
what_breaks_naive_approach: limit/offset can't express paging backward around a focal comment and drifts under inserts; returning the full list ships 3MB on viral posts, freezing the mobile client while it parses 200k comments it will never render.

title: Version a date/time field from naive local strings to RFC-3339 UTC with a compatibility alias
business_context: A B2B scheduling API returns start_time as 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm' in the server's local timezone with no offset. Clients in other timezones mis-render appointments, and DST shifts caused double-booked slots. We move to RFC-3339 UTC timestamps while keeping the legacy field readable for 120 integrations.
constraints: new field carries timezone (RFC 3339, UTC)
legacy naive-local field stays for current clients
no ambiguity across DST boundaries
both fields derive from one canonical stored UTC value
version pin selects which field is authoritative

before_state.description: Naive local datetime string with no offset; ambiguous across timezones and DST.

issues: No timezone offset: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm' is ambiguous
DST transitions shift wall-clock interpretation -> double bookings
Clients in other zones render the wrong local time

reasoning: Naive approach: append the server's offset to the existing string — rejected because the string format changes for all 120 clients and DST still makes a fixed offset wrong across the year. Rejected alternative: just switch start_time to UTC ISO — rejected because clients parsing the naive format break. Chosen: store canonical UTC, emit a new start_at (RFC 3339 UTC) and keep legacy start_time, both derived from the same value; version pin decides which is authoritative. Stored UTC -> serializer emits start_at (ISO Z) always + start_time (legacy local) -> new clients read start_at -> config sets canonical zone for legacy formatting -> tests assert DST correctness -> rollout deprecates start_time.

after_state.description: Canonical UTC drives both a new RFC-3339 start_at and the legacy local start_time; new clients use the unambiguous field.

files.purpose: Derives both the new UTC field and the legacy local string from one canonical UTC value, DST-correct.
Accepts the new offset-aware field or the legacy local string, normalizing both to canonical UTC on write.

migration_strategy: Add start_at (RFC 3339 UTC) to all responses alongside legacy start_time (additive)
Accept start_at on writes, interpreting legacy start_time in the canonical zone
Document that start_at is authoritative and start_time is deprecated
Migrate clients to read/write start_at; emit Deprecation on start_time usage
Audit for any remaining double-booking around DST; expect zero
Remove start_time in a future major after clients migrate
testing_strategy: Fixture: a slot stored at 2026-11-01T06:00:00Z (a US DST fall-back date). Assert start_at=='2026-11-01T06:00:00.000Z' and start_time reflects the correct canonical-zone wall clock for that instant (DST-aware). Round-trip: parse start_at back to the same UTC instant. DST test: two slots one hour apart across the fall-back boundary serialize to distinct, correct local strings. Chaos: write with start_time only -> interpreted in canonical zone; write with a bad start_at offset -> 400 invalid start_at.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Appending a fixed server offset to the naive string is wrong half the year because the offset changes at DST; clients in other timezones still can't reliably convert, and the DST wall-clock ambiguity keeps producing the double-booked slots.

title: Add a typed problem catalog and machine error codes to a gRPC API via rich error details
business_context: A fintech gRPC API returns only a status code + free-text message. Clients can't programmatically distinguish 'insufficient funds' from 'account frozen' (both INVALID_ARGUMENT), so they parse English strings that break on copy edits. We add google.rpc error details with stable codes.
constraints: stable machine-readable error codes clients switch on
keep gRPC status codes semantically correct
localizable message separate from the code
details must survive the gRPC trailer transport
no breaking change to the success responses

before_state.description: Errors carry only a status code and English text; clients parse strings.

issues: Both errors are InvalidArgument; clients can't distinguish programmatically
Message is English free text; copy edits break client parsing
No structured reason/domain/metadata

reasoning: Naive approach: invent a new gRPC status code per error — rejected because gRPC status codes are a fixed small set and overloading them breaks semantics. Rejected alternative: stuff a JSON blob into the message string — rejected because it's non-standard and tooling can't read it. Chosen: attach google.rpc.ErrorInfo (reason + domain + metadata) as status details with a stable reason enum. Server builds an ErrorInfo with reason='INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS' -> attaches via status.WithDetails -> client reads details, switches on reason -> localizable message stays in the message field -> config holds the catalog -> tests assert details survive transport -> rollout additive (success unchanged).

after_state.description: Errors carry google.rpc.ErrorInfo with a stable reason code and metadata; status codes stay correct.

files.purpose: Builds gRPC errors with a stable machine reason + domain + metadata while keeping the right status code.
Returns distinguishable, machine-switchable errors with correct FailedPrecondition status.
Client reads the stable reason from status details and switches on it instead of parsing English.

migration_strategy: Define the reason catalog (INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS, ACCOUNT_FROZEN, ...) in shared docs
Attach ErrorInfo details on the server (additive; status code corrected to FailedPrecondition)
Publish the catalog and client helper for reading details
Migrate clients to switch on reason instead of message text
Add a CI check that every returned error uses a catalog reason
Keep the human message localizable and free to edit without breaking clients
testing_strategy: Fixture: a from-account with low balance and a separate frozen account. Assert Transfer on low balance returns status FailedPrecondition with ErrorInfo.Reason=='INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS' and metadata.account set; frozen returns Reason=='ACCOUNT_FROZEN'. Transport test: round-trip the error through a real gRPC call and assert details survive the trailers (reasonOf returns the code). Stability test: change the human message text and assert the reason code is unchanged. Chaos: an error built without details -> reasonOf returns '' and the client falls back to a generic handler, no panic.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With both failures as INVALID_ARGUMENT and only English text, a client distinguishing them must string-match 'insufficient funds'; a copy edit to 'Insufficient balance' silently breaks every client's branch, and they can never reliably tell a retryable frozen-account case from a hard funds failure.

title: Make a DELETE endpoint soft-delete-aware with a versioned 'include_deleted' contract
business_context: A B2B SaaS DELETE /documents/{id} hard-deletes, but legal needs 7-year retention and users want an undo. We switch to soft delete with a deleted_at marker, hide soft-deleted docs from default reads, and add a versioned include_deleted flag for admins, without breaking clients expecting 204 on delete.
constraints: DELETE keeps returning 204 (no breaking change)
default reads exclude soft-deleted docs
admins can list deleted via an opt-in flag (authz-gated)
unique constraints must not block re-creating a name after soft delete
Postgres; existing rows have no deleted_at

before_state.description: Hard delete removes rows permanently; no retention or undo.

issues: Hard delete violates 7-year retention
No undo for accidental deletes
Reads would include deleted rows if we just stopped deleting

reasoning: Naive approach: stop deleting and add a status='deleted' column read everywhere — rejected because every existing query must remember to filter, and one forgotten filter leaks deleted docs. Rejected alternative: move deleted rows to an archive table on delete — rejected because cross-table reads and restores get complex and break referential integrity. Chosen: deleted_at soft-delete column with a partial unique index, default reads filtered, and a versioned admin include_deleted flag. DELETE sets deleted_at (still 204) -> default GET adds WHERE deleted_at IS NULL -> admin with flag sees deleted -> partial unique index allows name reuse -> config gates the flag by role -> tests assert hiding + retention -> rollout backfills nothing (NULL = active).

after_state.description: Soft delete via deleted_at; default reads exclude deleted; admins opt into include_deleted; partial unique index frees names.

files.purpose: Adds the soft-delete marker and a partial unique index so a name can be reused after deletion.
DELETE soft-deletes (still 204), reads default-exclude deleted, admins opt in, and restore provides undo.

migration_strategy: Add deleted_at + partial unique index; existing rows default NULL (active)
Switch DELETE to set deleted_at; default reads add deleted_at IS NULL
Add the admin include_deleted flag (authz-gated) and a restore endpoint
Add a retention job that hard-purges rows past the 7-year window
Verify no default-read path leaks deleted rows (audit queries)
Rollback = revert DELETE to soft-only off (no data loss, rows still present)
testing_strategy: Fixture: a document 'report.pdf'. DELETE -> assert 204 and the row now has deleted_at set (not removed). Assert default GET excludes it; admin GET ?include_deleted=true shows it; non-admin with the flag gets 403. Name reuse: create a new 'report.pdf' for the same owner and assert it succeeds (partial unique). Restore: POST /restore -> deleted_at NULL and it reappears in default reads. Chaos: DELETE an already-deleted doc -> 204 idempotent, no double-mark; retention purge removes only rows older than 7 years.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A status column read everywhere relies on every query remembering the filter; the first forgotten WHERE leaks deleted documents to users, and the original full unique(owner,name) constraint blocks re-creating a document with the same name after deletion.

title: Introduce API key scopes + version in the token to gate access to a new beta endpoint family
business_context: A devtools company is launching a beta /v3 analytics API. They want only opted-in keys to reach it, want to throttle beta separately, and need to avoid leaking beta to GA clients. Keys today are scopeless. We embed scopes + an api tier in the key record and gate beta routes.
constraints: GA keys must not reach beta endpoints
beta access is per-key opt-in
beta has a separate rate limit
key validation stays O(1) (no extra DB call per request)
backward compatible: existing keys keep GA access

before_state.description: Keys carry no scope/tier; any valid key reaches any route.

issues: No scopes/tier on the key; can't gate beta
All valid keys reach every route including future /v3
No per-tier rate limiting

reasoning: Naive approach: check a separate beta_allowlist table per request — rejected because it adds a DB lookup on the hot path. Rejected alternative: deploy beta on a separate domain with separate keys — rejected because it fragments the key model and clients juggle two credentials. Chosen: store scopes + tier on the cached key record and add a requireScope middleware; beta routes require scope 'beta:analytics' and use a beta rate-limit bucket. Request -> key middleware loads cached key with scopes/tier -> beta route's requireScope checks 'beta:analytics' -> beta limiter applies -> config maps tiers->limits -> tests assert GA keys 403 on beta -> rollout opts keys in by adding the scope.

after_state.description: Keys carry scopes + tier in the cached record; beta routes require a beta scope and a separate limiter.

files.purpose: Loads the cached key including scopes/tier so authorization needs no extra DB call.
Gates a route on a specific scope (e.g. beta:analytics) using the already-loaded key.
Beta route family requires the beta scope and applies an isolated rate limit.

migration_strategy: Add scopes + tier columns; backfill existing keys with GA scopes only
Update the key cache to include scopes/tier
Deploy requireScope + beta routes (no GA key has beta scope yet)
Opt selected keys into beta by adding 'beta:analytics' scope
Apply the beta rate limit and monitor beta usage separately
Promote beta to GA later by granting the scope broadly or dropping the gate
testing_strategy: Fixture: GA key (scopes:['read:analytics']) and beta key (scopes:['read:analytics','beta:analytics']). Assert GA key -> /v3/analytics/summary returns 403 insufficient_scope; beta key returns 200. Rate test: beta key hitting the beta endpoint is limited by the beta bucket (100/60s), independent of GA limits. Side-effect: assert no extra DB call on the authz path (key comes from cache). Chaos: a key with the beta scope but revoked tier still 403s if tier check added; cache miss -> 401, never open access.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A per-request beta_allowlist lookup adds a DB round trip to every call (including GA traffic) hurting latency at scale; without scope gating, the moment /v3 deploys, every existing GA key can reach the unstable beta endpoints and trip its separate limits.

title: Add a stable public schema layer over a Hasura/auto-generated CRUD API
business_context: A startup exposed its Postgres directly via an auto-generated GraphQL CRUD layer. Now 90 partners query tables directly, so any schema migration (renaming a column, splitting a table) breaks them. We introduce a curated public schema (views + a thin gateway) decoupled from the physical tables.
constraints: public schema stable across physical migrations
partners query only the public layer, never base tables
column renames/table splits invisible to partners
no N+1 from the abstraction
Postgres views/functions; read-heavy

before_state.description: Partners query base tables directly; physical migrations break them.

issues: Partners bind to the physical customers table and its column names
Renaming full_name -> name breaks every partner query
Splitting customers into customers+contacts is impossible without breakage

reasoning: Naive approach: rename columns carefully and tell partners to update — rejected because 90 partners can't update in lockstep and any rename breaks live queries. Rejected alternative: freeze the schema forever — rejected because the product must evolve the physical model. Chosen: a curated public schema (a separate Postgres schema of views) that maps stable public names to whatever the physical tables become; partners get permissions only on the views. Partner query -> hits api.customers view -> view selects/renames from physical tables -> migrations change the view body, not the public contract -> config grants only view access -> tests assert rename invisibility -> rollout repoints partner role to views.

after_state.description: A stable 'api' schema of views maps public names onto evolving physical tables; partners are granted only the views.

files.purpose: Defines the stable public view and restricts partners to the api schema, never the physical tables.
Demonstrates a physical rename absorbed by updating the view, keeping api.customers.name stable.
Tracks the api views (not base tables) so the GraphQL layer exposes only the stable public schema.

migration_strategy: Create the api schema with views mirroring current partner-visible columns
Grant partner_role SELECT on api views; revoke direct base-table access
Repoint Hasura partner permissions to the api schema
Verify partner queries return identical results via the views
Perform a physical rename, updating only the view to prove invisibility
Continue evolving physical tables freely; views are the contract
testing_strategy: Fixture: a customer row. Assert partner query of api.customers returns {id,name,email,created_at} with name from full_name. Migration test: run 0051 renaming full_name->display_name and assert the SAME partner query still returns name unchanged (contract stable). Authz test: partner_role SELECT on public.customers is denied (revoked). Performance: assert the view query plan is a simple projection (no extra join -> no N+1). Chaos: split customers into customers+contacts later, redefine the view with a join, and assert api.customers.email still resolves.
what_breaks_naive_approach: With partners bound to physical columns, renaming full_name or splitting the table fails every partner query the instant the migration lands; coordinating 90 partners to change column references simultaneously is impossible, so the physical schema is effectively frozen.
